```md
# attend

Local web dashboard for brief-based AI session management across Claude Code + Codex.

> session = cache, brief = state. vendor = replaceable backend, vault = substrate.

## What it does

- Scans your vaults for `brief.md` files (one per task)
- Reads Claude Code JSONL transcripts and matches them to briefs via `cwd`
- Derives per-brief telemetry: sessions / prompts / actions / dwell / last touch
- Classifies each brief into a behavioral pattern (avoidance / stalled / active / healthy / fresh)
- Ranks briefs by `memory-alignment ├Ќ pattern ├Ќ explicit blocker`
- Serves one localhost page you can poll instead of N IDE tabs

## Quick start

`attend` is a zero-install Node CLI Рђћ it boots a local web server and opens your browser. No app to download.

\```bash
# run against the current directory (and below) without installing anything
npx attend

# scan specific vault roots, pick a port
npx attend "D:\workspace\projects" "~/OneDrive/notes" --port 5050
\```

Run straight from the repo (no npm publish required):

\```bash
npx github:<you>/attend
\```

Or clone and run locally:

\```bash
git clone <repo> attend && cd attend
npm install
npm start              # = node dist/cli.js
\```

Then open `http://localhost:5050` (opened automatically unless `--no-open`).

> Requires Node РЅЦ 20. Works on Windows and macOS Рђћ paths and defaults are platform-aware.

### Publishing (optional, later)

The package is already structured for npm (`bin` entry + `dist/` build). If you ever want the short `npx attend` form to resolve from the public registry, that's a one-time `npm publish`. Until then, GitHub-`npx` and local `npm start` both work unchanged.

## CLI / config

\```
attend [dirs...] [options]

  dirs                 Vault roots to scan for brief.md (default: current dir)
  -p, --port <n>       Port (default: 5050)
      --host <addr>    Host to bind (default: 127.0.0.1)
  -c, --config <path>  Path to attend.config.json
      --no-open        Don't open the browser
  -h, --help           Help
\```

**Precedence:** CLI args > env > config file > platform defaults.

Env vars: `ATTEND_VAULTS` (path-separator-delimited), `ATTEND_PORT`, `ATTEND_HOST`, `ATTEND_CLAUDE_PROJECTS`, `ATTEND_CODEX_SESSIONS`.

Optional `attend.config.json` (in the cwd, or via `--config`):

\```json
{
  "vaultRoots": ["D:\\workspace\\projects", "C:\\Users\\you\\OneDrive\\notes"],
  "claudeProjects": "C:\\Users\\you\\.claude\\projects",
  "codexSessions": "C:\\Users\\you\\.codex\\sessions",
  "memorySources": [],
  "port": 5050
}
\```

`memorySources` empty Рєњ per-project Claude memory (`~/.claude/projects/*/memory/MEMORY.md`) is auto-discovered and unioned. Same memory model as Claude Code.

## Brief format

Each task gets a `brief.md` at `projects/<name>/brief.md`:

\```markdown
---
status: active            # active | deferred | done
last_touch: 2026-05-30
defer_until:              # condition, not date Рђћ e.g. "уГЅ X тЏътцЇ"
---

## what
СИђтЈЦУ»Ю, тіа why (СИ║С╗ђС╣ѕУ┐ЎС║Ітђ╝тЙЌтЂџ).

## accept
done уџётѕцТЇ« (СИЇТў» todo list).

## next
уј░тюетЇАтюетЊф / СИІСИђТГЦУ»ЦУ»ЋС╗ђС╣ѕ.
Т»ЈТгАуд╗т╝ђ session тЅЇТЏ┤Тќ░У┐ЎСИђУАї.
\```

Only `what` / `accept` / `next` sections are read. Other sections are ignored.

## Pattern definitions

| Pattern | Trigger | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| `fresh` | 0 sessions | Brief exists, no work started |
| `avoidance` | РЅЦ5 prompts, 0 actions | Many entries without productive output Рђћ possible decision avoidance |
| `stalled` | 0 actions, last touch РЅЦ 7 days | Cold, no recent activity |
| `healthy` | actions > 0, avg dwell РЅЦ 10 min, touch РЅц 3 days | In flow, don't interrupt |
| `active` | some actions, otherwise unclassified | Generic in-progress |

Telemetry is **descriptive, never judgmental** (Steel 2007 on procrastination self-report Рєњ avoidance feedback loops). All pattern labels are observations, not verdicts.

## Priority scoring (v1 heuristic)

`score = (memory alignment ├Ќ 2) + pattern weight + explicit blocker bonus Рѕњ defer/done penalty`

Each brief gets a one-line reason explaining the rank. Reasons compose so you can override.

## Spawn commands

Detail page generates copy-paste commands for both Claude and Codex.
**Does not auto-spawn** Рђћ it copies to clipboard, you paste to your terminal.
This is intentional: auto-spawn from dashboard Рєњ 4-tab cascade.

## Architecture

\```
src/
  cli.ts            CLI entry: parse args, resolve config, boot server, open browser
  server.ts         Hono HTTP server: feed + detail routes (server-rendered HTML)
  config.ts         config resolution + platform defaults
  core/             pure domain logic (no server deps) Рђћ unit-tested
    brief.ts        parse brief.md + scan vaults
    vendor/         SessionSource interface + claude (JSONL) + codex (stub)
    telemetry.ts    cwd-containment match + aggregation
    pattern.ts      behavioral classifier
    memory.ts       Claude-convention per-project memory keywords
    priority.ts     scoring + reasons
  ui/               server-rendered feed/detail HTML
legacy/             original Python/Flask daemon (reference)
\```

`core/` is decoupled from the server so it's testable in isolation. New vendors plug in via the `SessionSource` interface (`src/core/vendor/`) Рђћ everything downstream is vendor-neutral.

## Develop

\```bash
npm run dev          # tsx, no build
npm test             # vitest
npm run typecheck    # tsc --noEmit
npm run lint         # biome
npm run build        # tsup Рєњ dist/cli.js
\```

## What this is NOT (deliberately)

- Not a chat UI Рђћ sessions stay in Claude Code / Codex
- Not push notifications Рђћ pull, manual refresh
- Not a TODO app Рђћ briefs are task-shaped, not item-shaped
- Not multi-user Рђћ single-person attention router
- Not a downloadable native app Рђћ zero-install via `npx`, runs in your browser

## Design invariants

Touch these only with cause (see `DESIGN.md`):

1. **brief = state, session = cache** Рђћ nothing the dashboard knows lives only in a session
2. **pull, not push** Рђћ no notifications unless user opts in later
3. **descriptive telemetry, never judgmental** Рђћ Steel constraint
4. **vendor-neutral data, vendor-locked execution** Рђћ JSONL is read; spawn is per-vendor
5. **single polling surface** Рђћ adding a second dashboard is a regression

# CLAUDE.md

This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.

## Onboarding

`DESIGN.md` is the source of truth for *why* this exists Рђћ read it end-to-end, including the **"v1: Node/npx pivot"** section, which records why this was rewritten from Python/Flask to a Node CLI and why Tauri was rejected. `brief.md` holds the current `next` step. `README.md` has the user-facing contract and the file map.

As of v1 (2026-05) `attend` is a **Node + TypeScript** package, distributed zero-install via `npx` (boots a local web server, opens the browser). The original Python implementation lives in `legacy/` for reference only Рђћ do not extend it.

## Commands

\```bash
npm install
npm run dev          # tsx Рђћ run from source, no build
npm start            # node dist/cli.js (requires build first)
npm test             # vitest
npx vitest run test/priority.test.ts   # single test file
npm run typecheck    # tsc --noEmit
npm run lint         # biome check src test  (use --write to fix)
npm run build        # tsup Рєњ dist/cli.js (single bundled ESM file)
\```

Run the CLI directly: `node dist/cli.js [dirs...] --no-open --port 5071`. Requires Node РЅЦ 20.

## Architecture

The pipeline, all faithfully ported from `legacy/daemon.py`:

1. **`src/config.ts`** Рђћ `resolveConfig()` merges CLI args > env > `attend.config.json` > platform defaults (`os.homedir()`-based, no hardcoded paths). This is how "ТїЄт«џуЏ«тйЋ" works.
2. **`src/core/brief.ts`** Рђћ `scanVault()` recursively finds `brief.md`; `parseBriefText()` splits flat YAML front matter + `## what/accept/next` sections (a tiny parser, no YAML dep Рђћ the format is flat).
3. **`src/core/vendor/`** Рђћ the extension seam. `SessionSource` interface (`index.ts`) with `ClaudeSource` (JSONL parse) and `CodexSource` (deliberate empty stub Рђћ Codex schema unknown; never fabricate data). `collectSessions()` unions all sources. **A new vendor = one new `SessionSource` impl; nothing downstream changes.**
4. **`src/core/telemetry.ts`** Рђћ `matchSessions()` ties sessions to a brief by **bidirectional cwd containment** (normalizes `\` vs `/`); `telemetryForBrief()` aggregates sessions/prompts/actions/dwell/ages.
5. **`src/core/pattern.ts`** / **`priority.ts`** Рђћ classifier + `score + composed reason`. `memory.ts` provides keywords: per-project Claude memory (`~/.claude/projects/*/memory/MEMORY.md`), auto-discovered and unioned Рђћ same memory model as Claude Code.
6. **`src/server.ts`** (Hono) renders feed (`/`) + detail (`/brief?path=`) as server-rendered HTML from `src/ui/`. Sessions cached 30s, memory keywords 60s, to keep refresh cheap.

`src/core/` has **no server dependency** Рђћ that's what makes it unit-testable and is the key structural invariant. Keep vendor-specific logic confined to `src/core/vendor/` and spawn-command emission (`core/spawn.ts`); everything else is vendor-neutral.

## Design invariants (do not violate without explicit cause; see DESIGN.md)

1. **brief = state, session = cache** Рђћ nothing the dashboard knows may live only in a session.
2. **pull, not push** Рђћ no notifications/SSE/websockets.
3. **descriptive telemetry, never judgmental** Рђћ pattern labels and reason strings must be observation-form, never second-person pressure. Steel 2007. Making output more "motivating" is a regression. The Codex stub returning nothing (vs. fake data) is part of this.
4. **vendor-neutral data, vendor-locked execution** Рђћ keep vendor logic inside `core/vendor/` and `core/spawn.ts`.
5. **single polling surface** Рђћ one localhost page. Zero-install via `npx`, browser is the UI; do NOT turn this into a downloadable native app (that's why Tauri was rejected Рђћ see DESIGN.md).

## Conventions

- Strict TS, ESM, `verbatimModuleSyntax` (use `import type` for type-only imports). Imports use `.js` extensions.
- Format/lint via biome (`biome.json`); 100-col, double quotes, 2-space.
- Tests are pure (no fs/network) Рђћ pass a fixed `now` to time-dependent functions like `telemetryForBrief`.

**Structure** (the elegant/extensible part):

- `src/cli.ts` + `config.ts` Рђћ `npx` entry; **ТїЄт«џуЏ«тйЋ** via positional args / `attend.config.json` / env, with platform defaults from `os.homedir()` (no hardcoded paths).

You don't need to publish to npm. The package is structured so all three work: `npx github:you/attend` (zero cost, just push), `npm publish` Рєњ `npx attend` (optional, free for public), or local `npm start`.

```text
1	# attend Рђћ design
2	
3	This document captures the design context that led to v0. Subsequent sessions opened in this project should read this first to onboard.
4	
5	## Problem
6	
7	A single person collaborating with AI faces four compounding costs:
8	
9	1. **Concurrency** Рђћ 10+ active sessions across Claude Code (multiple VS Code projects) + Codex CLI is normal, not pathological. Reducing the count is not the goal.
10	2. **Vendor isolation** Рђћ Claude Code and Codex are separate ecosystems. `/fork` exists in each (Codex CLI has it, Claude Code CLI has it but the VS Code panel does not). Neither is cross-vendor. Manual copy-paste is the current state.
11	3. **Decision avoidance** Рђћ when a session needs a hard decision, the user switches to easier completed sessions to "verify and push." Classic Steel-type *avoidant procrastination*. Self-report doesn't detect it (gets rationalized as "efficient batching"). Behavioral telemetry would.
12	4. **Polling overhead** Рђћ N IDE tabs ├Ќ re-orientation cost on each switch (UC Irvine: 23 min 15 s to refocus; APA: 40% productivity loss from switching). Adding more organization tools makes this worse, not better.
13	
14	## Conceptual model Рђћ the invariant
15	
16	```
17	session = cache       (transient, vendor-bound, may die)
18	brief   = state       (persistent, vendor-neutral, survives session death)
19	vendor  = replaceable backend
20	vault   = substrate
21	```
22	
23	A task may use 0..N sessions over its life. Sessions are vehicles; briefs are passengers. **A cold 3-day-old session is usually more expensive to resume than spawning a fresh session from a brief** Рђћ chat history accumulates dead context, summarization loses fidelity, and the user's own working memory of the session has decayed.
24	
25	Concrete consequence: don't try to keep sessions alive across attention gaps. Externalize state to brief; kill the session; spawn fresh when ready.
26	
27	## Four components
28	
29	### 1. Brief
30	
31	- **Granularity**: per-task, not per-session
32	- **Location**: `projects/<name>/brief.md` Рђћ physically colocated with work, not in a separate "briefs/" directory. Found by `ls`, not by remembering.
33	- **Three sections**:
34	  - `what` Рђћ one sentence + *why* this is worth doing
35	  - `accept` Рђћ done criteria (not a TODO list)
36	  - `next` Рђћ **where you are stuck / what to try next**. Update this line before every session-leave. Gollwitzer's implementation-intention research: the meta-decision ("what should I do?") is where cost concentrates; pre-committing it at cheap time pays off massively.
37	- **Front matter**: `status` (active/deferred/done), `defer_until` (a *condition*, not a date Рђћ "уГЅ X тЏътцЇ"), `last_touch`
38	- **Lifecycle**: created manually or via split; killed only by explicit `status: done`. Cold sessions are killed; their briefs persist.
39	
40	### 2. Telemetry
41	
42	- **Data sources**: vendor JSONL transcripts
43	  - Claude Code: `~/.claude/projects/<encoded-cwd>/*.jsonl`
44	  - Codex CLI: `~/.codex/sessions/YYYY/MM/DD/*.jsonl` (not parsed in v0)
45	- **Session-to-brief matching**: by `cwd` field in JSONL. A session's cwd contains (or equals) the brief's project directory.
46	- **Per-brief signals**: sessions count, prompts count, action count (Edit/Write/Bash tool uses), dwell distribution, last_touch_age, last_action_age
47	- **Pattern classifier**:
48	
49	| Pattern | Trigger | What it means |
50	|---|---|---|
51	| `fresh` | 0 sessions | Brief exists, no work started |
52	| `avoidance` | РЅЦ5 prompts, 0 actions | Many entries, no productive output Рђћ decision is what's needed, not more "work" |
53	| `stalled` | 0 actions, last touch РЅЦ 7 days | Cold Рђћ needs unblock or kill |
54	| `healthy` | actions > 0, avg dwell РЅЦ 10 min, touch РЅц 3 days | In flow, don't interrupt |
55	| `active` | actions > 0, otherwise | Generic in-progress |
56	
57	- **Hard constraint** Рђћ descriptive, never judgmental (Steel 2007: judgmental feedback on procrastination behavior *worsens* it via self-esteem mediation). Output strings must avoid second-person pressure verbs ("you again," "still stuck"). Use observation-form: "X count, Y duration, Z actions."
58	
59	### 3. Priority evaluator
60	
61	- **Inputs**: brief ├Ќ user-memory keywords (extracted from MEMORY.md) ├Ќ telemetry pattern ├Ќ explicit blockers in `next`
62	- **Output**: ranked list + **one-line reason per rank** (no opaque scores; user must be able to override)
63	- **Time NOT used as input** Рђћ explicitly rejected (see below). Semantic alignment with memory is the real axis; pattern signals action implication.
64	- v0: heuristic scoring (memory keyword hits ├Ќ 2 + pattern bonus + blocker bonus Рѕњ defer penalty)
65	- v1+: LLM-based, env-flagged (`ATTEND_LLM=anthropic|openai`)
66	
67	### 4. Split (not in v0)
68	
69	- **Purpose**: when a session organically grows N follow-up threads, factor them out into N new briefs
70	- **Flow**: read transcript Рєњ LLM "identify open follow-up threads" Рєњ N candidate briefs in 3-line format Рєњ user reviews Рєњ commits selected to vault
71	- **Intentional limitation**: does NOT auto-spawn sessions. spawn is a separate user decision. Auto-spawn from split would create a 4-tab cascade Рђћ exactly the load problem we're solving.
72	- **Distinction from fork**: fork = duplicate-and-diverge (Warp, Claude SDK, Codex CLI); split = factor-out. Fork copies state; split extracts task specs. They're different operations.
73	- **Cross-vendor**: split reads JSONL on disk Рђћ vendor-neutral input Рєњ vendor-neutral brief output. Vendor only re-enters at spawn time.
74	
75	## Product form
76	
77	**Local web app at `http://localhost:5050`. Python + Flask + server-side HTML.** Daemon scans vault, reads vendor JSONL, renders ranked feed. Polled by browser refresh.
78	
79	### Why this form
80	
81	- **Single polling surface** Рђћ one tab, one URL. Replaces N IDE tabs.
82	- **Vendor-neutral** Рђћ not bound to VS Code or any terminal. Treats Claude and Codex equally.
83	- **Data in local vault** Рђћ daemon is view-only; if it dies the vault is still authoritative.
84	- **Incremental enrichment** Рђћ start static HTML, add buttons (spawn, defer, split) as needed.
85	
86	### Forms rejected
87	
88	- **VS Code plugin** Рђћ locks one IDE; can't span Claude + Codex
89	- **TUI** Рђћ multi-window management collapses back to the tab-bounce problem
90	- **Desktop app** Рђћ engineering overhead without benefit
91	- **Push notifications** Рђћ user confirmed: when polling = idle moments, push doesn't help (can't act when not idle). Pull-only.
92	
93	### Inspirational reference
94	
95	Slock.ai's structural primitives Рђћ channel/thread (persistent, task-shaped) vs. message stream (transient) Рђћ directly informed brief/session separation. We use the structural insight without copying the multi-agent collaboration features.
96	
97	## Design invariants
98	
99	Touch these only with explicit cause:
100	
101	1. **brief = state, session = cache** Рђћ nothing the dashboard knows lives only in a session
102	2. **pull, not push** Рђћ no notifications unless user opts in
103	3. **descriptive telemetry, never judgmental** Рђћ Steel constraint, see telemetry section
104	4. **vendor-neutral data, vendor-locked execution** Рђћ JSONL is read; spawn is per-vendor
105	5. **single polling surface** Рђћ adding a second dashboard is a regression
106	
107	## Rejected paths (don't redo)
108	
109	- **4-bucket organization** (TODO / У┐ЏУАїСИГ / ти▓т«їТѕљ / жЋ┐ТюЪС┐ЮуЋЎ in separate locations). Asks user to remember N locations. Replaced by brief sidecar at task location, found by `ls`.
110	- **Obsidian bidirectional links / Zettelkasten-style PKM**. Explicitly deferred Рђћ user judgment was that base models + memory + agent platforms will deliver this upstream over time. Don't preempt; revisit if upstream stalls.
111	- **Hot/warm/cold by time**. Session state changes fast but total count stays high; recency doesn't track priority. Replaced by memory-aligned semantic priority + pattern-based action implication.
112	- **LangChain Agent Inbox / push notifications**. User's diagnostic cost when polling is already near-zero; push adds noise without enabling action.
113	- **Cap to 2-5 concurrent sessions**. Open РЅа active. Goal isn't reducing tab count, it's reducing re-warming cost via durable briefs.
114	- **Fork as cross-vendor primitive**. Each vendor's fork is locked to its transcript format and API. Brief is the cross-vendor abstraction; fork lives one level below.
115	- **Auto-spawn from dashboard**. Auto-spawning new sessions from priority list would recreate the cascade we just dismantled. Spawn buttons copy commands only.
116	
117	## Cross-vendor data layer
118	
119	Both vendors store transcripts as local JSONL with a `cwd` field. Daemon reads both, matches sessions to briefs by cwd-vs-project-dir containment, derives signals per brief, renders vendor-neutral output. Vendor only re-appears at:
120	
121	1. **Transcript ingestion** (per-vendor JSONL parser)
122	2. **Spawn command** (per-vendor CLI invocation)
123	
124	Everything in between (briefs, dashboard, priority, telemetry) is vendor-agnostic markdown/HTML.
125	
126	## Research touchstones
127	
128	- **Steel, P. (2007)** Рђћ *Arousal, avoidant, and decisional procrastinators: do they exist?* Three procrastination types; foundational for the avoidance pattern definition. Identified behavioral-measurement gap: most procrastination research is self-report, telemetry is largely unexplored. This is the niche the telemetry component fills.
129	- **Gollwitzer, P. (1999)** Рђћ *Implementation intentions.* Pre-committing "if X then Y" at cheap time reduces decision-point friction. Direct rationale for the `next` line discipline.
130	- **Rosenbaum (2014)** Рђћ *Pre-crastination.* Doing the easy thing first to free working memory is a real mechanism, not always avoidance. Distinguishes by whether the easy task ends in 1-2 hours; if longer, it's likely the avoidance pattern.
131	- **Karpathy LLM Wiki pattern** Рђћ `wiki/hot.md` of ~500 words auto-loaded at session start. Brief is our hot-cache analog.
132	- **Slock.ai** Рђћ Persistent channels with transient message streams. Structural inspiration for brief/session split.
133	
134	## v0 Рєњ v1 roadmap
135	
136	- [ ] Codex JSONL parser (need a sample of Codex's schema; user has it via CLI but not yet active)
137	- [ ] `attend split <jsonl-path>` CLI: LLM extracts open follow-up threads Рєњ N candidate briefs to review
138	- [ ] `attend new <project>` to scaffold `projects/<project>/brief.md`
139	- [ ] LLM-based priority option (env-flag)
140	- [ ] Refined blocker regex (only `уГЅ[тЁиСйЊтєЁт«╣]`, not bare `уГЅ` char Рђћ currently triggers false positives on parentheticals like "(СйютЊЂB уГЅ)")
141	- [ ] Dwell distribution heatmap per brief
142	- [ ] In-browser brief edit (currently file-system only)
143	
144	## Out of scope (likely permanently)
145	
146	- Multi-user collaboration (Slock-style server/channel/DM)
147	- Real-time push / SSE / websocket
148	- Chat UI in the dashboard (sessions live in Claude Code / Codex)
149	- Hierarchical task trees (briefs are flat; relations live in the `next` line as references)
150	- Cloud sync (vault is local; OneDrive sync of the vault is incidental, not a feature)
151	
152	## How a future session onboards
153	
154	If you (Claude / Codex / human) are picking this up cold:
155	
156	1. Read this document end-to-end (including the v1 pivot section below).
157	2. Read `README.md` for run instructions.
158	3. Read `brief.md` for current `next`.
159	4. Open http://localhost:5050 if the app is running (`npx attend` or `npm start`).
160	5. Pick from the brief's `next` and start.
161	
162	Do not add organizational layers unless you've checked them against invariant 5. Do not change telemetry output to be more "motivating" Рђћ that's invariant 3 violation; see Steel research.
163	
164	## v1: Node/npx pivot (2026-05)
165	
166	v0 was a single-file Python/Flask `daemon.py` run via `python daemon.py`. v1 reimplements it as a **Node + TypeScript** package distributable via `npx` (zero-install: `npx attend` / `npx github:you/attend`, boots a local server, opens the browser). The original is preserved under `legacy/` for reference.
167	
168	**Why the change.** The goal was cross-platform (Win + macOS) zero-install distribution. Two product forms were weighed:
169	
170	- **Tauri (rejected).** Considered, then rejected: Tauri bundles a native installer you *download and install* Рђћ the opposite of zero-install. It also renders into an embedded WebView, not a localhost page you open in any browser, so it would have broken the "single localhost polling surface" form. (Note: this supersedes the older "desktop app Рђћ engineering overhead" rejection note above for the same reason, just made concrete.)
171	- **Node CLI + local web server (chosen).** Matches `npx serve`-style distribution: publish once (or run from GitHub), `npx` fetches-and-runs, no permanent install, browser is the UI. Node runs on Win/macOS uniformly. This *restores* the original localhost single-surface form Рђћ only the runtime changed (Python Рєњ Node), to make `npx` distribution possible.
172	
173	**What did NOT change.** All five design invariants hold. The memory model is unchanged Рђћ still Claude Code's per-project memory (`~/.claude/projects/*/memory/MEMORY.md`), just generalized from one hardcoded file to auto-discovering and unioning all per-project memories. The pattern definitions, priority heuristic, descriptive-telemetry constraint, and spawn-by-copy behavior are ported 1:1 (one refinement: the bare-"уГЅ" blocker false-positive from the roadmap is fixed in `priority.ts`).
174	
175	**Architecture.** `src/core/` is pure domain logic with no server dependency (unit-tested with vitest); `src/server.ts` (Hono) and `src/ui/` are a thin render layer; `src/core/vendor/` holds the `SessionSource` extension point (Claude impl + Codex stub). Adding a vendor = one new `SessionSource` Рђћ the rest stays vendor-neutral (invariant 4). See `README.md` for the file map and dev commands.
176
```
</details></tool-use>

```text
1	# attend
2	
3	Local web dashboard for brief-based AI session management across Claude Code + Codex.
4	
5	> session = cache, brief = state. vendor = replaceable backend, vault = substrate.
6	
7	## What it does
8	
9	- Scans your vaults for `brief.md` files (one per task)
10	- Reads Claude Code JSONL transcripts and matches them to briefs via `cwd`
11	- Derives per-brief telemetry: sessions / prompts / actions / dwell / last touch
12	- Classifies each brief into a behavioral pattern (avoidance / stalled / active / healthy / fresh)
13	- Ranks briefs by `memory-alignment ├Ќ pattern ├Ќ explicit blocker`
14	- Serves one localhost page you can poll instead of N IDE tabs
15	
16	## Quick start
17	
18	`attend` is a zero-install Node CLI Рђћ it boots a local web server and opens your browser. No app to download.
19	
20	```bash
21	# run against the current directory (and below) without installing anything
22	npx attend
23	
24	# scan specific vault roots, pick a port
25	npx attend "D:\workspace\projects" "~/OneDrive/notes" --port 5050
26	```
27	
28	Run straight from the repo (no npm publish required):
29	
30	```bash
31	npx github:<you>/attend
32	```
33	
34	Or clone and run locally:
35	
36	```bash
37	git clone <repo> attend && cd attend
38	npm install
39	npm start              # = node dist/cli.js
40	```
41	
42	Then open `http://localhost:5050` (opened automatically unless `--no-open`).
43	
44	> Requires Node РЅЦ 20. Works on Windows and macOS Рђћ paths and defaults are platform-aware.
45	
46	### Publishing (optional, later)
47	
48	The package is already structured for npm (`bin` entry + `dist/` build). If you ever want the short `npx attend` form to resolve from the public registry, that's a one-time `npm publish`. Until then, GitHub-`npx` and local `npm start` both work unchanged.
49	
50	## CLI / config
51	
52	```
53	attend [dirs...] [options]
54	
55	  dirs                 Vault roots to scan for brief.md (default: current dir)
56	  -p, --port <n>       Port (default: 5050)
57	      --host <addr>    Host to bind (default: 127.0.0.1)
58	  -c, --config <path>  Path to attend.config.json
59	      --no-open        Don't open the browser
60	  -h, --help           Help
61	```
62	
63	**Precedence:** CLI args > env > config file > platform defaults.
64	
65	Env vars: `ATTEND_VAULTS` (path-separator-delimited), `ATTEND_PORT`, `ATTEND_HOST`, `ATTEND_CLAUDE_PROJECTS`, `ATTEND_CODEX_SESSIONS`.
66	
67	Optional `attend.config.json` (in the cwd, or via `--config`):
68	
69	```json
70	{
71	  "vaultRoots": ["D:\\workspace\\projects", "C:\\Users\\you\\OneDrive\\notes"],
72	  "claudeProjects": "C:\\Users\\you\\.claude\\projects",
73	  "codexSessions": "C:\\Users\\you\\.codex\\sessions",
74	  "memorySources": [],
75	  "port": 5050
76	}
77	```
78	
79	`memorySources` empty Рєњ per-project Claude memory (`~/.claude/projects/*/memory/MEMORY.md`) is auto-discovered and unioned. Same memory model as Claude Code.
80	
81	## Brief format
82	
83	Each task gets a `brief.md` at `projects/<name>/brief.md`:
84	
85	```markdown
86	---
87	status: active            # active | deferred | done
88	last_touch: 2026-05-30
89	defer_until:              # condition, not date Рђћ e.g. "уГЅ X тЏътцЇ"
90	---
91	
92	## what
93	СИђтЈЦУ»Ю, тіа why (СИ║С╗ђС╣ѕУ┐ЎС║Ітђ╝тЙЌтЂџ).
94	
95	## accept
96	done уџётѕцТЇ« (СИЇТў» todo list).
97	
98	## next
99	уј░тюетЇАтюетЊф / СИІСИђТГЦУ»ЦУ»ЋС╗ђС╣ѕ.
100	Т»ЈТгАуд╗т╝ђ session тЅЇТЏ┤Тќ░У┐ЎСИђУАї.
101	```
102	
103	Only `what` / `accept` / `next` sections are read. Other sections are ignored.
104	
105	## Pattern definitions
106	
107	| Pattern | Trigger | Meaning |
108	|---|---|---|
109	| `fresh` | 0 sessions | Brief exists, no work started |
110	| `avoidance` | РЅЦ5 prompts, 0 actions | Many entries without productive output Рђћ possible decision avoidance |
111	| `stalled` | 0 actions, last touch РЅЦ 7 days | Cold, no recent activity |
112	| `healthy` | actions > 0, avg dwell РЅЦ 10 min, touch РЅц 3 days | In flow, don't interrupt |
113	| `active` | some actions, otherwise unclassified | Generic in-progress |
114	
115	Telemetry is **descriptive, never judgmental** (Steel 2007 on procrastination self-report Рєњ avoidance feedback loops). All pattern labels are observations, not verdicts.
116	
117	## Priority scoring (v1 heuristic)
118	
119	`score = (memory alignment ├Ќ 2) + pattern weight + explicit blocker bonus Рѕњ defer/done penalty`
120	
121	Each brief gets a one-line reason explaining the rank. Reasons compose so you can override.
122	
123	## Spawn commands
124	
125	Detail page generates copy-paste commands for both Claude and Codex.
126	**Does not auto-spawn** Рђћ it copies to clipboard, you paste to your terminal.
127	This is intentional: auto-spawn from dashboard Рєњ 4-tab cascade.
128	
129	## Architecture
130	
131	```
132	src/
133	  cli.ts            CLI entry: parse args, resolve config, boot server, open browser
134	  server.ts         Hono HTTP server: feed + detail routes (server-rendered HTML)
135	  config.ts         config resolution + platform defaults
136	  core/             pure domain logic (no server deps) Рђћ unit-tested
137	    brief.ts        parse brief.md + scan vaults
138	    vendor/         SessionSource interface + claude (JSONL) + codex (stub)
139	    telemetry.ts    cwd-containment match + aggregation
140	    pattern.ts      behavioral classifier
141	    memory.ts       Claude-convention per-project memory keywords
142	    priority.ts     scoring + reasons
143	  ui/               server-rendered feed/detail HTML
144	legacy/             original Python/Flask daemon (reference)
145	```
146	
147	`core/` is decoupled from the server so it's testable in isolation. New vendors plug in via the `SessionSource` interface (`src/core/vendor/`) Рђћ everything downstream is vendor-neutral.
148	
149	## Develop
150	
151	```bash
152	npm run dev          # tsx, no build
153	npm test             # vitest
154	npm run typecheck    # tsc --noEmit
155	npm run lint         # biome
156	npm run build        # tsup Рєњ dist/cli.js
157	```
158	
159	## What this is NOT (deliberately)
160	
161	- Not a chat UI Рђћ sessions stay in Claude Code / Codex
162	- Not push notifications Рђћ pull, manual refresh
163	- Not a TODO app Рђћ briefs are task-shaped, not item-shaped
164	- Not multi-user Рђћ single-person attention router
165	- Not a downloadable native app Рђћ zero-install via `npx`, runs in your browser
166	
167	## Design invariants
168	
169	Touch these only with cause (see `DESIGN.md`):
170	
171	1. **brief = state, session = cache** Рђћ nothing the dashboard knows lives only in a session
172	2. **pull, not push** Рђћ no notifications unless user opts in later
173	3. **descriptive telemetry, never judgmental** Рђћ Steel constraint
174	4. **vendor-neutral data, vendor-locked execution** Рђћ JSONL is read; spawn is per-vendor
175	5. **single polling surface** Рђћ adding a second dashboard is a regression
176
```

Based on the search results, here's what I found about Claude Squad and managing multiple AI coding agents with terminal/tmux:

## Claude Squad Overview

Claude Squad is a terminal app that manages multiple Claude Code, Codex, Gemini (and other local agents including Aider) in separate workspaces, allowing you to work on multiple tasks simultaneously.

## Key Features

It manages multiple agents in one TUI, gives each task an isolated git workspace, and supports agents such as Claude Code, Codex, Gemini, Aider, OpenCode, and Amp through configurable launch commands. You can complete tasks in the background (including yolo / auto-accept mode!)

## How It Works with Tmux

Claude Squad uses tmux to create isolated terminal sessions for each agent and git worktrees to isolate codebases. It gives you a dedicated terminal app to launch, supervise, pause, review, and ship multiple AI coding agents across isolated workspaces.

## Installation

Both Homebrew and manual installation will install Claude Squad as cs on your system. You can install via Homebrew or using a curl script that puts the cs binary in ~/.local/bin.

## Prerequisites

Prerequisites include tmux and gh (GitHub CLI).

## Alternative: Claude Code Agent Teams

The search results also reveal that Claude Code Agent Teams coordinate multiple Claude Code instances working together as a team, with shared tasks, inter-agent messaging, and centralized management. Split panes require tmux or iTerm2: the default in-process mode works in any terminal.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about Vibe Kanban and its role in managing multiple AI coding agents orchestration in 2025:

## Overview

Vibe Kanban is an open-source software project launched in 2025 by BloopAI, designed as a terminal-based Kanban board for managing and monitoring multiple AI coding agents.

## Core Functionality

Vibe Kanban orchestrates multiple AI coding agents, tracks tasks, and manages your development workflow efficiently. The killer feature of vibe-kanban is isolated parallel executionРђћevery task runs in its own git worktree, preventing agents from interfering with each other or your main branch.

## Agent Support

The platform works with coding agents like Claude Code, Gemini CLI and Amp. Each workspace gets its own terminal session where you can invoke any coding agent from the supported list (Claude Code, Codex, Gemini CLI, GitHub Copilot, Amp, Cursor, OpenCode, Droid, CCR, and Qwen CodeРђћ10+ agents total).

## Technical Architecture

Vibe Kanban is a dual-process application: a Rust backend handles git operations, workspace management, and terminal sessions, while a React frontend provides the kanban board and review interface. The two communicate via HTTP APIs, with optional relay/tunnel mode for remote server deployments.

## Adoption & Impact

With 9.4k GitHub stars, it's becoming the standard for orchestrating AI coding workflows. Additionally, Vibe Kanban is sunsetting as a commercial product and will continue as open source and community maintained.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about Conductor, Crystal, Claude Code, multiple sessions, and git worktree:

## Key Tools and Applications

**Conductor**
Conductor.build is a free Mac app that runs multiple Claude Code sessions in parallel, each in an isolated git worktree. Conductor spins up a git worktree, runs your setup script, and auto-names the branch, with you looking at a fresh isolated checkout in about 10 seconds.

**Crystal (Deprecated)**
Crystal (formerly a multi-session AI code assistant manager) has been deprecated and replaced by Nimbalyst as of February 2026. However, you can continue using Crystal, but we recommend moving to Nimbalyst for active updates.

## Git Worktrees with Claude Code

The desktop app creates a worktree for every new session automatically. Running each Claude Code session in its own worktree means edits in one session never touch files in another, so you can have Claude building a feature in one terminal while fixing a bug in a second.

To run Claude Code in its own git worktree, just start it with the --worktree option, allowing you to run multiple parallel Claude Code sessions in the same git repo without the code edits clobbering each other.

## The Conductor Workflow

Conductor ships a /resolve-merge-conflicts slash command for resolving conflicts when running multiple workspaces in parallel.

```text
1	# attend Рђћ design
2	
3	This document captures the design context that led to v0. Subsequent sessions opened in this project should read this first to onboard.
4	
5	## Problem
6	
7	A single person collaborating with AI faces four compounding costs:
8	
9	1. **Concurrency** Рђћ 10+ active sessions across Claude Code (multiple VS Code projects) + Codex CLI is normal, not pathological. Reducing the count is not the goal.
10	2. **Vendor isolation** Рђћ Claude Code and Codex are separate ecosystems. `/fork` exists in each (Codex CLI has it, Claude Code CLI has it but the VS Code panel does not). Neither is cross-vendor. Manual copy-paste is the current state.
11	3. **Decision avoidance** Рђћ when a session needs a hard decision, the user switches to easier completed sessions to "verify and push." Classic Steel-type *avoidant procrastination*. Self-report doesn't detect it (gets rationalized as "efficient batching"). Behavioral telemetry would.
12	4. **Polling overhead** Рђћ N IDE tabs ├Ќ re-orientation cost on each switch (UC Irvine: 23 min 15 s to refocus; APA: 40% productivity loss from switching). Adding more organization tools makes this worse, not better.
13	
14	## Conceptual model Рђћ the invariant
15	
16	```
17	session = cache       (transient, vendor-bound, may die)
18	brief   = state       (persistent, vendor-neutral, survives session death)
19	vendor  = replaceable backend
20	vault   = substrate
21	```
22	
23	A task may use 0..N sessions over its life. Sessions are vehicles; briefs are passengers. **A cold 3-day-old session is usually more expensive to resume than spawning a fresh session from a brief** Рђћ chat history accumulates dead context, summarization loses fidelity, and the user's own working memory of the session has decayed.
24	
25	Concrete consequence: don't try to keep sessions alive across attention gaps. Externalize state to brief; kill the session; spawn fresh when ready.
26	
27	## Four components
28	
29	### 1. Brief
30	
31	- **Granularity**: per-task, not per-session
32	- **Location**: `projects/<name>/brief.md` Рђћ physically colocated with work, not in a separate "briefs/" directory. Found by `ls`, not by remembering.
33	- **Three sections**:
34	  - `what` Рђћ one sentence + *why* this is worth doing
35	  - `accept` Рђћ done criteria (not a TODO list)
36	  - `next` Рђћ **where you are stuck / what to try next**. Update this line before every session-leave. Gollwitzer's implementation-intention research: the meta-decision ("what should I do?") is where cost concentrates; pre-committing it at cheap time pays off massively.
37	- **Front matter**: `status` (active/deferred/done), `defer_until` (a *condition*, not a date Рђћ "уГЅ X тЏътцЇ"), `last_touch`
38	- **Lifecycle**: created manually or via split; killed only by explicit `status: done`. Cold sessions are killed; their briefs persist.
39	
40	### 2. Telemetry
41	
42	- **Data sources**: vendor JSONL transcripts
43	  - Claude Code: `~/.claude/projects/<encoded-cwd>/*.jsonl`
44	  - Codex CLI: `~/.codex/sessions/YYYY/MM/DD/*.jsonl` (not parsed in v0)
45	- **Session-to-brief matching**: by `cwd` field in JSONL. A session's cwd contains (or equals) the brief's project directory.
46	- **Per-brief signals**: sessions count, prompts count, action count (Edit/Write/Bash tool uses), dwell distribution, last_touch_age, last_action_age
47	- **Pattern classifier**:
48	
49	| Pattern | Trigger | What it means |
50	|---|---|---|
51	| `fresh` | 0 sessions | Brief exists, no work started |
52	| `avoidance` | РЅЦ5 prompts, 0 actions | Many entries, no productive output Рђћ decision is what's needed, not more "work" |
53	| `stalled` | 0 actions, last touch РЅЦ 7 days | Cold Рђћ needs unblock or kill |
54	| `healthy` | actions > 0, avg dwell РЅЦ 10 min, touch РЅц 3 days | In flow, don't interrupt |
55	| `active` | actions > 0, otherwise | Generic in-progress |
56	
57	- **Hard constraint** Рђћ descriptive, never judgmental (Steel 2007: judgmental feedback on procrastination behavior *worsens* it via self-esteem mediation). Output strings must avoid second-person pressure verbs ("you again," "still stuck"). Use observation-form: "X count, Y duration, Z actions."
58	
59	### 3. Priority evaluator
60	
61	- **Inputs**: brief ├Ќ user-memory keywords (extracted from MEMORY.md) ├Ќ telemetry pattern ├Ќ explicit blockers in `next`
62	- **Output**: ranked list + **one-line reason per rank** (no opaque scores; user must be able to override)
63	- **Time NOT used as input** Рђћ explicitly rejected (see below). Semantic alignment with memory is the real axis; pattern signals action implication.
64	- v0: heuristic scoring (memory keyword hits ├Ќ 2 + pattern bonus + blocker bonus Рѕњ defer penalty)
65	- v1+: LLM-based, env-flagged (`ATTEND_LLM=anthropic|openai`)
66	
67	### 4. Split (not in v0)
68	
69	- **Purpose**: when a session organically grows N follow-up threads, factor them out into N new briefs
70	- **Flow**: read transcript Рєњ LLM "identify open follow-up threads" Рєњ N candidate briefs in 3-line format Рєњ user reviews Рєњ commits selected to vault
71	- **Intentional limitation**: does NOT auto-spawn sessions. spawn is a separate user decision. Auto-spawn from split would create a 4-tab cascade Рђћ exactly the load problem we're solving.
72	- **Distinction from fork**: fork = duplicate-and-diverge (Warp, Claude SDK, Codex CLI); split = factor-out. Fork copies state; split extracts task specs. They're different operations.
73	- **Cross-vendor**: split reads JSONL on disk Рђћ vendor-neutral input Рєњ vendor-neutral brief output. Vendor only re-enters at spawn time.
74	
75	## Product form
76	
77	**Local web app at `http://localhost:5050`. Python + Flask + server-side HTML.** Daemon scans vault, reads vendor JSONL, renders ranked feed. Polled by browser refresh.
78	
79	### Why this form
80	
81	- **Single polling surface** Рђћ one tab, one URL. Replaces N IDE tabs.
82	- **Vendor-neutral** Рђћ not bound to VS Code or any terminal. Treats Claude and Codex equally.
83	- **Data in local vault** Рђћ daemon is view-only; if it dies the vault is still authoritative.
84	- **Incremental enrichment** Рђћ start static HTML, add buttons (spawn, defer, split) as needed.
85	
86	### Forms rejected
87	
88	- **VS Code plugin** Рђћ locks one IDE; can't span Claude + Codex
89	- **TUI** Рђћ multi-window management collapses back to the tab-bounce problem
90	- **Desktop app** Рђћ engineering overhead without benefit
91	- **Push notifications** Рђћ user confirmed: when polling = idle moments, push doesn't help (can't act when not idle). Pull-only.
92	
93	### Inspirational reference
94	
95	Slock.ai's structural primitives Рђћ channel/thread (persistent, task-shaped) vs. message stream (transient) Рђћ directly informed brief/session separation. We use the structural insight without copying the multi-agent collaboration features.
96	
97	## Design invariants
98	
99	Touch these only with explicit cause:
100	
101	1. **brief = state, session = cache** Рђћ nothing the dashboard knows lives only in a session
102	2. **pull, not push** Рђћ no notifications unless user opts in
103	3. **descriptive telemetry, never judgmental** Рђћ Steel constraint, see telemetry section
104	4. **vendor-neutral data, vendor-locked execution** Рђћ JSONL is read; spawn is per-vendor
105	5. **single polling surface** Рђћ adding a second dashboard is a regression
106	
107	## Rejected paths (don't redo)
108	
109	- **4-bucket organization** (TODO / У┐ЏУАїСИГ / ти▓т«їТѕљ / жЋ┐ТюЪС┐ЮуЋЎ in separate locations). Asks user to remember N locations. Replaced by brief sidecar at task location, found by `ls`.
110	- **Obsidian bidirectional links / Zettelkasten-style PKM**. Explicitly deferred Рђћ user judgment was that base models + memory + agent platforms will deliver this upstream over time. Don't preempt; revisit if upstream stalls.
111	- **Hot/warm/cold by time**. Session state changes fast but total count stays high; recency doesn't track priority. Replaced by memory-aligned semantic priority + pattern-based action implication.
112	- **LangChain Agent Inbox / push notifications**. User's diagnostic cost when polling is already near-zero; push adds noise without enabling action.
113	- **Cap to 2-5 concurrent sessions**. Open РЅа active. Goal isn't reducing tab count, it's reducing re-warming cost via durable briefs.
114	- **Fork as cross-vendor primitive**. Each vendor's fork is locked to its transcript format and API. Brief is the cross-vendor abstraction; fork lives one level below.
115	- **Auto-spawn from dashboard**. Auto-spawning new sessions from priority list would recreate the cascade we just dismantled. Spawn buttons copy commands only.
116	
117	## Cross-vendor data layer
118	
119	Both vendors store transcripts as local JSONL with a `cwd` field. Daemon reads both, matches sessions to briefs by cwd-vs-project-dir containment, derives signals per brief, renders vendor-neutral output. Vendor only re-appears at:
120	
121	1. **Transcript ingestion** (per-vendor JSONL parser)
122	2. **Spawn command** (per-vendor CLI invocation)
123	
124	Everything in between (briefs, dashboard, priority, telemetry) is vendor-agnostic markdown/HTML.
125	
126	## Research touchstones
127	
128	- **Steel, P. (2007)** Рђћ *Arousal, avoidant, and decisional procrastinators: do they exist?* Three procrastination types; foundational for the avoidance pattern definition. Identified behavioral-measurement gap: most procrastination research is self-report, telemetry is largely unexplored. This is the niche the telemetry component fills.
129	- **Gollwitzer, P. (1999)** Рђћ *Implementation intentions.* Pre-committing "if X then Y" at cheap time reduces decision-point friction. Direct rationale for the `next` line discipline.
130	- **Rosenbaum (2014)** Рђћ *Pre-crastination.* Doing the easy thing first to free working memory is a real mechanism, not always avoidance. Distinguishes by whether the easy task ends in 1-2 hours; if longer, it's likely the avoidance pattern.
131	- **Karpathy LLM Wiki pattern** Рђћ `wiki/hot.md` of ~500 words auto-loaded at session start. Brief is our hot-cache analog.
132	- **Slock.ai** Рђћ Persistent channels with transient message streams. Structural inspiration for brief/session split.
133	
134	## v0 Рєњ v1 roadmap
135	
136	- [ ] Codex JSONL parser (need a sample of Codex's schema; user has it via CLI but not yet active)
137	- [ ] `attend split <jsonl-path>` CLI: LLM extracts open follow-up threads Рєњ N candidate briefs to review
138	- [ ] `attend new <project>` to scaffold `projects/<project>/brief.md`
139	- [ ] LLM-based priority option (env-flag)
140	- [ ] Refined blocker regex (only `уГЅ[тЁиСйЊтєЁт«╣]`, not bare `уГЅ` char Рђћ currently triggers false positives on parentheticals like "(СйютЊЂB уГЅ)")
141	- [ ] Dwell distribution heatmap per brief
142	- [ ] In-browser brief edit (currently file-system only)
143	
144	## Out of scope (likely permanently)
145	
146	- Multi-user collaboration (Slock-style server/channel/DM)
147	- Real-time push / SSE / websocket
148	- Chat UI in the dashboard (sessions live in Claude Code / Codex)
149	- Hierarchical task trees (briefs are flat; relations live in the `next` line as references)
150	- Cloud sync (vault is local; OneDrive sync of the vault is incidental, not a feature)
151	
152	## How a future session onboards
153	
154	If you (Claude / Codex / human) are picking this up cold:
155	
156	1. Read this document end-to-end (including the v1 pivot section below).
157	2. Read `README.md` for run instructions.
158	3. Read `brief.md` for current `next`.
159	4. Open http://localhost:5050 if the app is running (`npx attend` or `npm start`).
160	5. Pick from the brief's `next` and start.
161	
162	Do not add organizational layers unless you've checked them against invariant 5. Do not change telemetry output to be more "motivating" Рђћ that's invariant 3 violation; see Steel research.
163	
164	## v1: Node/npx pivot (2026-05)
165	
166	v0 was a single-file Python/Flask `daemon.py` run via `python daemon.py`. v1 reimplements it as a **Node + TypeScript** package distributable via `npx` (zero-install: `npx attend` / `npx github:you/attend`, boots a local server, opens the browser). The original is preserved under `legacy/` for reference.
167	
168	**Why the change.** The goal was cross-platform (Win + macOS) zero-install distribution. Two product forms were weighed:
169	
170	- **Tauri (rejected).** Considered, then rejected: Tauri bundles a native installer you *download and install* Рђћ the opposite of zero-install. It also renders into an embedded WebView, not a localhost page you open in any browser, so it would have broken the "single localhost polling surface" form. (Note: this supersedes the older "desktop app Рђћ engineering overhead" rejection note above for the same reason, just made concrete.)
171	- **Node CLI + local web server (chosen).** Matches `npx serve`-style distribution: publish once (or run from GitHub), `npx` fetches-and-runs, no permanent install, browser is the UI. Node runs on Win/macOS uniformly. This *restores* the original localhost single-surface form Рђћ only the runtime changed (Python Рєњ Node), to make `npx` distribution possible.
172	
173	**What did NOT change.** All five design invariants hold. The memory model is unchanged Рђћ still Claude Code's per-project memory (`~/.claude/projects/*/memory/MEMORY.md`), just generalized from one hardcoded file to auto-discovering and unioning all per-project memories. The pattern definitions, priority heuristic, descriptive-telemetry constraint, and spawn-by-copy behavior are ported 1:1 (one refinement: the bare-"уГЅ" blocker false-positive from the roadmap is fixed in `priority.ts`).
174	
175	**Architecture.** `src/core/` is pure domain logic with no server dependency (unit-tested with vitest); `src/server.ts` (Hono) and `src/ui/` are a thin render layer; `src/core/vendor/` holds the `SessionSource` extension point (Claude impl + Codex stub). Adding a vendor = one new `SessionSource` Рђћ the rest stays vendor-neutral (invariant 4). See `README.md` for the file map and dev commands.
176
```

1	# attend
2	
3	Local web dashboard for brief-based AI session management across Claude Code + Codex.
4	
5	> session = cache, brief = state. vendor = replaceable backend, vault = substrate.
6	
7	## What it does
8	
9	- Scans your vaults for `brief.md` files (one per task)
10	- Reads Claude Code JSONL transcripts and matches them to briefs via `cwd`
11	- Derives per-brief telemetry: sessions / prompts / actions / dwell / last touch
12	- Classifies each brief into a behavioral pattern (avoidance / stalled / active / healthy / fresh)
13	- Ranks briefs by `memory-alignment ├Ќ pattern ├Ќ explicit blocker`
14	- Serves one localhost page you can poll instead of N IDE tabs
15	
16	## Quick start
17	
18	`attend` is a zero-install Node CLI Рђћ it boots a local web server and opens your browser. No app to download.
19	
20	```bash
21	# run against the current directory (and below) without installing anything
22	npx attend
23	
24	# scan specific vault roots, pick a port
25	npx attend "D:\workspace\projects" "~/OneDrive/notes" --port 5050
26	```
27	
28	Run straight from the repo (no npm publish required):
29	
30	```bash
31	npx github:<you>/attend
32	```
33	
34	Or clone and run locally:
35	
36	```bash
37	git clone <repo> attend && cd attend
38	npm install
39	npm start              # = node dist/cli.js
40	```
41	
42	Then open `http://localhost:5050` (opened automatically unless `--no-open`).
43	
44	> Requires Node РЅЦ 20. Works on Windows and macOS Рђћ paths and defaults are platform-aware.
45	
46	### Publishing (optional, later)
47	
48	The package is already structured for npm (`bin` entry + `dist/` build). If you ever want the short `npx attend` form to resolve from the public registry, that's a one-time `npm publish`. Until then, GitHub-`npx` and local `npm start` both work unchanged.
49	
50	## CLI / config
51	
52	```
53	attend [dirs...] [options]
54	
55	  dirs                 Vault roots to scan for brief.md (default: current dir)
56	  -p, --port <n>       Port (default: 5050)
57	      --host <addr>    Host to bind (default: 127.0.0.1)
58	  -c, --config <path>  Path to attend.config.json
59	      --no-open        Don't open the browser
60	  -h, --help           Help
61	```
62	
63	**Precedence:** CLI args > env > config file > platform defaults.
64	
65	Env vars: `ATTEND_VAULTS` (path-separator-delimited), `ATTEND_PORT`, `ATTEND_HOST`, `ATTEND_CLAUDE_PROJECTS`, `ATTEND_CODEX_SESSIONS`.
66	
67	Optional `attend.config.json` (in the cwd, or via `--config`):
68	
69	```json
70	{
71	  "vaultRoots": ["D:\\workspace\\projects", "C:\\Users\\you\\OneDrive\\notes"],
72	  "claudeProjects": "C:\\Users\\you\\.claude\\projects",
73	  "codexSessions": "C:\\Users\\you\\.codex\\sessions",
74	  "memorySources": [],
75	  "port": 5050
76	}
77	```
78	
79	`memorySources` empty Рєњ per-project Claude memory (`~/.claude/projects/*/memory/MEMORY.md`) is auto-discovered and unioned. Same memory model as Claude Code.
80	
81	## Brief format
82	
83	Each task gets a `brief.md` at `projects/<name>/brief.md`:
84	
85	```markdown
86	---
87	status: active            # active | deferred | done
88	last_touch: 2026-05-30
89	defer_until:              # condition, not date Рђћ e.g. "уГЅ X тЏътцЇ"
90	---
91	
92	## what
93	СИђтЈЦУ»Ю, тіа why (СИ║С╗ђС╣ѕУ┐ЎС║Ітђ╝тЙЌтЂџ).
94	
95	## accept
96	done уџётѕцТЇ« (СИЇТў» todo list).
97	
98	## next
99	уј░тюетЇАтюетЊф / СИІСИђТГЦУ»ЦУ»ЋС╗ђС╣ѕ.
100	Т»ЈТгАуд╗т╝ђ session тЅЇТЏ┤Тќ░У┐ЎСИђУАї.
101	```
102	
103	Only `what` / `accept` / `next` sections are read. Other sections are ignored.
104	
105	## Pattern definitions
106	
107	| Pattern | Trigger | Meaning |
108	|---|---|---|
109	| `fresh` | 0 sessions | Brief exists, no work started |
110	| `avoidance` | РЅЦ5 prompts, 0 actions | Many entries without productive output Рђћ possible decision avoidance |
111	| `stalled` | 0 actions, last touch РЅЦ 7 days | Cold, no recent activity |
112	| `healthy` | actions > 0, avg dwell РЅЦ 10 min, touch РЅц 3 days | In flow, don't interrupt |
113	| `active` | some actions, otherwise unclassified | Generic in-progress |
114	
115	Telemetry is **descriptive, never judgmental** (Steel 2007 on procrastination self-report Рєњ avoidance feedback loops). All pattern labels are observations, not verdicts.
116	
117	## Priority scoring (v1 heuristic)
118	
119	`score = (memory alignment ├Ќ 2) + pattern weight + explicit blocker bonus Рѕњ defer/done penalty`
120	
121	Each brief gets a one-line reason explaining the rank. Reasons compose so you can override.
122	
123	## Spawn commands
124	
125	Detail page generates copy-paste commands for both Claude and Codex.
126	**Does not auto-spawn** Рђћ it copies to clipboard, you paste to your terminal.
127	This is intentional: auto-spawn from dashboard Рєњ 4-tab cascade.
128	
129	## Architecture
130	
131	```
132	src/
133	  cli.ts            CLI entry: parse args, resolve config, boot server, open browser
134	  server.ts         Hono HTTP server: feed + detail routes (server-rendered HTML)
135	  config.ts         config resolution + platform defaults
136	  core/             pure domain logic (no server deps) Рђћ unit-tested
137	    brief.ts        parse brief.md + scan vaults
138	    vendor/         SessionSource interface + claude (JSONL) + codex (stub)
139	    telemetry.ts    cwd-containment match + aggregation
140	    pattern.ts      behavioral classifier
141	    memory.ts       Claude-convention per-project memory keywords
142	    priority.ts     scoring + reasons
143	  ui/               server-rendered feed/detail HTML
144	legacy/             original Python/Flask daemon (reference)
145	```
146	
147	`core/` is decoupled from the server so it's testable in isolation. New vendors plug in via the `SessionSource` interface (`src/core/vendor/`) Рђћ everything downstream is vendor-neutral.
148	
149	## Develop
150	
151	```bash
152	npm run dev          # tsx, no build
153	npm test             # vitest
154	npm run typecheck    # tsc --noEmit
155	npm run lint         # biome
156	npm run build        # tsup Рєњ dist/cli.js
157	```
158	
159	## What this is NOT (deliberately)
160	
161	- Not a chat UI Рђћ sessions stay in Claude Code / Codex
162	- Not push notifications Рђћ pull, manual refresh
163	- Not a TODO app Рђћ briefs are task-shaped, not item-shaped
164	- Not multi-user Рђћ single-person attention router
165	- Not a downloadable native app Рђћ zero-install via `npx`, runs in your browser
166	
167	## Design invariants
168	
169	Touch these only with cause (see `DESIGN.md`):
170	
171	1. **brief = state, session = cache** Рђћ nothing the dashboard knows lives only in a session
172	2. **pull, not push** Рђћ no notifications unless user opts in later
173	3. **descriptive telemetry, never judgmental** Рђћ Steel constraint
174	4. **vendor-neutral data, vendor-locked execution** Рђћ JSONL is read; spawn is per-vendor
175	5. **single polling surface** Рђћ adding a second dashboard is a regression
176
```
</details></tool-use>

Based on the search results, here's what I found about parallel AI coding agents orchestration with git worktrees and Claude Code in 2025:

## Key Orchestration Tools & Frameworks

Agent Orchestrator manages fleets of AI coding agents working in parallel on your codebase, with each agent getting its own git worktree, its own branch, and its own PR. It's agent-agnostic (Claude Code, Codex, Aider) and runtime-agnostic (tmux, ConPTY/process, Docker).

Parallel Code allows you to dispatch AI coding agents in parallel, each in its own worktree, enabling five agents on five features at the same time with zero conflicts.

ccswarm is a Rust-native multi-agent orchestration framework that coordinates specialized AI agents using Claude Code CLI and provides task delegation infrastructure with Git worktree isolation for parallel development.

## Claude Code's Native Worktree Support

A git worktree is a separate working directory with its own files and branch, and running each Claude Code session in its own worktree means edits in one session never touch files in another. Subagents can run in their own worktrees so parallel edits don't conflict, either by asking Claude to "use worktrees for your agents" or setting it permanently on a custom subagent.

## Why Worktrees Matter for Parallel Agents

When running two Claude Code sessions against the same working directory, agents overwrite each other's edits to shared files and the dev server only runs on one portРђћworktrees fix the file isolation problem while database branching and port assignment fix the rest.

## Advanced Orchestration Patterns

Parallel development with git worktrees involves running multiple Claude agents simultaneously on different branches of your codebase using custom slash commands, with each agent working independently on its own branch to produce different implementations.

Several tools have appeared to address orchestration challenges, including agentree, git-worktree-runner, worktree-cli, and ccswarm for coordinating multiple agents with specialized pools in worktree-isolated environments.

## Multi-Agent Coding Tools Overview

The top tools for parallel AI coding agents on git worktrees in 2026 include Nimbalyst, Conductor, Vibe Kanban, Claude Squad, and Crystal.

### Claude Squad
Claude Squad provides zero-setup terminal parallelism for managing multiple AI agents. It manages multiple agents in one TUI, gives each task an isolated git workspace, and supports agents such as Claude Code, Codex, Gemini, Aider, OpenCode, and Amp through configurable launch commands.

### Conductor
Conductor is the fastest way to start multi-agent orchestration on Mac. It runs multiple Claude Code and Codex agents in parallel, each in its own git worktree, with a visual dashboard and diff-first review UI.

### Vibe Kanban
Vibe Kanban is available as open source and self-hosting. However, Bloop, the company behind Vibe Kanban, shut down in April 2026 and the project transitioned to community-maintained open source.

### Crystal
Crystal enables developers to run multiple Claude Code AI sessions simultaneously with isolated git worktrees for each session. Features persistent conversation tracking, built-in git operations, change visualization, and desktop notifications for streamlined parallel AI-assisted development workflows.

### Nimbalyst
Nimbalyst is a visual workspace with kanban for tasks and sessions that also handles mockups, diagrams, data models, and multi-editor workflows with heterogeneous agents.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about Karpathy's LLM Wiki pattern and the "hot.md" context file concept:

## Karpathy LLM Wiki Pattern

On April 4, 2026, Andrej Karpathy published a GitHub gist titled "llm-wiki" Рђћ an idea file for building personal knowledge bases on top of LLMs.

## Three-Layer Architecture

The architecture has three layers Рђћ raw sources (immutable), the wiki (LLM-owned), and a schema file like CLAUDE.md or AGENTS.md (the conventions).

## Hot Cache/Hot.md Pattern

Regarding the specific "hot.md" file you mentioned, one implementation adds a "hot cache" layer Рђћ at the end of every session it updates a recent-context summary so the next session resumes with full context, no recap needed. This is the "hot" context file pattern Рђћ a recent-context summary that persists session-to-session knowledge without requiring recaps.

## Core Concept

The LLM Wiki pattern says: instead of relying only on RAG or chat history, let AI continuously maintain a structured, interlinked wiki that compounds over time. The LLM compiles raw sources into a structured, persistent, interlinked Markdown wiki Рђћ and then queries the wiki instead of the raw sources.

The pattern has gained significant adoption, with the gist crossing 5,000 stars within two weeks, with hundreds of comments, dozens of GitHub re-implementations, and write-ups across VentureBeat, Medium, and Substack.

## Search Results Summary

I found comprehensive information about LangChain Agent Inbox, human-in-the-loop patterns, and how to implement interrupts with multiple agents. Here are the key findings:

### Agent Inbox Overview
Agent Inbox is an inbox UX for interacting with human-in-the-loop agents. To set it up, you need to provide the Assistant/Graph ID (the name of your LangGraph graph), and the Deployment URL (how Agent Inbox connects to your LangGraph deployment for fetching interrupts and sending human responses).

### Human-in-the-Loop with Interrupts
The Human-in-the-Loop (HITL) middleware lets you add human oversight to agent tool calls. When a model proposes an action that might require reviewРђћfor example, writing to a file or executing SQLРђћthe middleware can pause execution and wait for a decision.

When multiple tool calls are paused at the same time, each action requires a separate decision. Decisions must be provided in the same order as the actions appear in the interrupt request.

### Interrupt Response Options
The four types of actions available are: accept (accept the interrupt's arguments), edit (edit the interrupt's arguments), response (send a response to the interrupt), and ignore.

### Multiple Agent Workflows
This design pattern is useful in an LLM application consisting of multiple agents. One or more agents may need to carry out multi-turn conversations with a human, where the human provides input or feedback at different stages of the conversation.

Based on the search results, here are the key findings about managing parallel AI coding agents and the context switching problem:

## The Core Problem

When running multiple AI coding agents in parallel, developers become the bottleneck, context-switching between agents, resolving merge conflicts they created, and manually checking if anything still compiles.

## The Broader Developer Bottleneck

Time saved writing code was consumed by coordination overhead: more context switching, managing higher volumes of changes, and keeping parallel work aligned. A 2025 analysis by Faros AI of over 10,000 developers found that teams with high AI adoption interacted with 9% more tasks and 47% more pull requests per day.

## What Actually Limits Shipping Speed

The bottlenecks are not typing speed, but rather design reviews, PR queues, test failures, context switching, and waiting on deployments. Additionally, AI-driven coding gains evaporate when review bottlenecks, brittle testing, and slow release pipelines can't match the new velocity.

## Technical Solutions Emerging

In September 2025, Anthropic announced context management capabilities for Claude Sonnet 4.5, with results showing that combining memory tools with context editing improved AI agent performance by 39% over baseline, with context editing alone delivering a 29% improvement.

Recent research introduced the Git-Context-Controller (GCC), which structures an agent's memory like software version control systems with operations like COMMIT, BRANCH, MERGE, and CONTEXT, allowing agents to commit important information, branch off to explore different approaches, and merge findings back together.

## Practical Constraints

The bottleneck isn't the agent or the modelРђћit's the human orchestrating one task at a time, and context switching between different types of changes is the biggest time sink in review.

Based on the web search results, I found relevant information about AI agent patterns and trends in 2026:

## Key 2026 Trends

Teams are deploying narrow, always-on workflows such as ops hubs, async research pipelines, and background automation, moving away from universal assistants.

Most teams that succeed start narrow, focus on always-on or async workflows, and avoid unnecessary orchestration or memory overhead.

## The Inbox Pattern Evolution

In 2025, teams learned to manage their inbox. In 2026, they're designing the email system itself. This reflects a shift from managing individual prompts toward designing the entire system architecture that consumes context.

Once an agent touches memory, inboxes, code execution, or multi-channel gateways, the differentiator shifts away from the model itself to the runtime: state management, permissions, observability, and failure handling.

## Coding Agent Trends

For AI coding agents specifically, AG2 (the v0.4 rewrite of AutoGen) features an event-driven core, async-first execution, and pluggable orchestration strategies. Additionally, coding agents increasingly focus on persistent workloads with memory, inboxes, and multi-channel gateways, where the runtime differentiator matters more than the model itself.

The overall 2026 trend emphasizes operational efficiency over architectural complexity, with a focus on longer-lived async agents that maintain state rather than numerous short-lived agents.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about Claude Code subagents, background tasks, Codex cloud async delegation, and related orchestration in 2026:

## Claude Code Subagents & Background Tasks

Claude Code subagents run in their own context window with a custom system prompt, specific tool access, and independent permissions. When Claude encounters a task that matches a subagent's description, it delegates to that subagent, which works independently and returns results.

Claude Code now supports async subagents where background agents keep working even after your main task completes, and wake up your main agent when they're done.

## Codex Cloud Async Delegation

OpenAI Codex (the 2026 version) is a cloud-native agentic environment, embedded inside ChatGPT, that spins up sandboxed containers per task and is optimized for async, fire-and-forget delegation. You describe a task, Codex spins up a cloud environment, runs the agent against your codebase, and returns a diff. You do not monitor the agent's reasoning in real time; you define the task upfront and collect results later.

## Orchestrator Pattern

The main agent acts as an orchestrator, delegating individual investigation and implementation tasks to subagents that each have their own context window. Exploration noise stays contained within the subagent; only a summary returns to the parent. Independent tasks run in parallel, and read-only subagents receive no write tools, enforcing least-privilege.

## Strategic Use in 2026

The recommended pattern is using Claude Code for local implementation, focused refactor loops, and policy-heavy code changes, while using Codex app for parallel background delegation, multi-thread work planning, and broad research or execution batches.

Here are the search results for **Codex CLI session resume management and multiple sessions for VS Code 2026**:

## Key Findings

### Session Resume Management

The resume subcommand lets you reopen an earlier thread with the same repository state and instructions. There are several ways to manage sessions:

- codex resume launches a picker of recent interactive sessions.
- codex resume --last skips the picker and jumps straight to your most recent session from the current working directory (add --all to ignore the current working directory filter).
- codex resume <SESSION_ID> targets a specific run.

### Multiple Session Management

A thread started in the CLI can be resumed in the Desktop app without any export/import dance Рђћ both connect to the same backend and the same persisted state. For automation, codex exec resume continues a session non-interactively with a follow-up prompt.

### Session Persistence

Every Codex CLI session is automatically saved as a JSONL file in ~/.codex/sessions/. Each file contains the full conversation transcript: your prompts, model responses, tool calls, and tool results, timestamped and structured for replay.

### VS Code Integration

For VS Code users working with multiple sessions, the Codex app now has built-in worktree support, skills, automations, and git functionality. This is stronger than bare thread persistence, but still not the same thing as a cross-tool session manager.

### Third-Party Session Managers

Several tools are available for enhanced multi-session management, including session dashboards and kanban boards for tracking parallel work across multiple agent sessions.

Based on the web search results, I found highly relevant information about parallel AI agents and review fatigue. Here's what I found:

## Review Fatigue with Parallel AI Agents

Parallel streams Рђћ such as authentication in one thread, billing in another, and infrastructure in a third Рђћ multiply the switching cost beyond what traditional development workflows demanded. Your attention depletes, your trust in the output inflates, and every context switch makes both worse, resulting in review fatigue: the point where sustained code review quietly shifts from deep evaluation to surface scanning.

## Solutions for Managing Verification Overhead

To address these challenges, there are emerging tools designed to manage the verification bottleneck. Some platforms solve the "verification bottleneck" by providing isolated environments for each agent, allowing you to monitor and validate parallel streams of AI-generated code without losing context.

One practical approach uses parallel subagents where each focused on a specific aspect of code quality analyzes the changes independently and returns findings ranked by severity, with the main agent then combining all results into a prioritized summary.

## Technical Challenges

Parallel AI agents produce emergent failures that no single agent's logs can explain. Most teams need structured logs with agent IDs and correlation IDs so failures can be attributed, and worktree isolation so parallel edits cannot corrupt each other.

## Windsurf Agent Command Center

Windsurf 2.0 introduced the Agent Command Center, a new surface for managing every agent you have running, both local and cloud, in one place. It is organized as a Kanban board grouped by status, so you can see at a glance what each agent is working on, what is blocked, and what is ready for review.

Tasks are cleanly organized into status columns like Running, Blocked, and Ready, and you can spin up multiple agents, watch their respective cards slide into the Running column, and track their progress at a glance without text cluttering your primary workspace.

## Cursor Background Agents

Cursor also supports background agents with status tracking. Recent updates fixed background task reliability issues like resume failures, interrupt edge cases, and incorrect status scoping on resumed subagents. Cursor added clearer status text for background/resumed tasks.

Additionally, Cursor's /loop feature allows agents to run a prompt repeatedly on a local schedule until a certain outcome is achieved, or until you stop it, with the agent deciding when or what event should wake it if you don't specify a fixed interval, for use with local long-running agents.

## Key Differences

Cursor 2.0 added parallel agent sessions for up to eight, while Windsurf does not have a comparable parallel agent dashboard.

Based on the search results, here are the key insights about AI coding agents, human review bottlenecks, context switching, and parallel agents in 2026:

## Human Review as a Bottleneck

Human review becomes the bottleneck when running multiple parallel AI coding agents. The bottleneck isn't the agent or the modelРђћit's the human orchestrating one task at a time.

## Context Switching in Review

Context switching between different types of changes is the biggest time sink in review. Research shows significant productivity impacts: adding a single extra project to a developer's workload consumes 20% of their time through context switching, and adding a third makes half their time evaporate.

## Parallel Agents Architecture

Tools like Superset IDE provide a unified dashboard showing all active agents with real-time status indicators, notification systems that alert when agents need attention, and built-in diff viewers with syntax highlighting and side-by-side comparisons. On a modern laptop, 5-7 concurrent agents are comfortable.

## Reviewing Diffs Efficiently

Rather than re-reading entire files, focus on what changedРђћif the diff is scoped to what you asked for and the tests pass, a quick review is usually sufficient. Batching similar tasks together and reviewing all test additions together before refactors reduces context switching overhead.

## Broader Context and Workflow Changes

The bottleneck is context: the gap between what engineers carry in their heads and what AI can understand or communicate. Looking forward, AI judges will assess pull request risk with enough accuracy to route changes intelligently, with background agents spinning up in the cloud to handle scoped work like fixing CI or adding unit tests.

I've completed the web search for your query about spec-driven development, durable state, markdown, AI coding agents, handoff, session resume, and 2026. Here are the key findings:

## Spec-Driven Development (SDD) in 2026

SDD emerged in 2025 as a direct response to the failure mode of "vibe coding" with large language models, and by 2026, every major AI coding toolРђћGitHub Spec Kit, AWS Kiro, Claude Code, Cursor, OpenSpec, BMAD, Tessl, and Google AntigravityРђћhas shipped its own flavor of SDD.

## Core Concepts

Spec-driven development involves writing a clear markdown spec defining what to build, and letting your coding agent implement it. Specs are treated as durable docs that outlive the code generated from them; future agents and humans read them as the canonical reference.

## Durable State and Session Resume

Specs preserve context across agent sessions, reduce cognitive debt, and improve intent fidelity, keeping your agent aligned with what you actually want. Structured artifacts provide persistent context for AI agents, and agents reload context each sessionРђћno more lost knowledge.

## Handoff Protocols

The plan becomes the handoff from human planning to agent execution. Each handoff requires file-based context passing, though strict access control can prevent smooth coordination; a living spec with a coordinator-based loop would handle feedback automatically.

## Markdown-Based Format

Markdown-based agents work anywhere with no vendor lock-in, and specs are IDE and AI-agnostic portable markdown files that work with any coding assistant.

Based on the web search results, here's relevant information about developer prioritization and managing abandoned side projects:

## Key Insights on Abandoned Projects

Quitting a side project is prioritizationРђћyou're choosing to spend your limited time and energy on something with a better return. Knowing when to abandon a project is a skill, and a really important one.

## Understanding the Difference Between Quitting and Failure

The side project graveyard becomes problematic only when it represents a pattern of unintentional abandonment РђЊ projects you wanted to finish but couldn't. Projects killed deliberately, after providing value (even just learning value), aren't failuresРђћthey're completed in a different way than originally envisioned.

## Systems for Managing Multiple Projects

A "Projects Parking Lot/Idea Graveyard" (a Trello board or Notion page) is a single place where you log every project and its current status, ensuring nothing feels lost.

Not every project deserves to be finished, and that's okay. Quitting a project isn't a failure, it's prioritizationРђћsome ideas were great in theory but didn't align with long-term goals.

## Questions to Ask Yourself

When deciding whether to quit, ask: "Am I quitting because it's hard or because it's pointless?" Hard is not a reason to quit, but if you've figured out what you came to learn but the project is no longer useful to you, it's time to move on.

## cmux (2026 Features)

cmux, created by the team at Manaflow AI, combines Ghostty's blazing-fast GPU-accelerated rendering with a sidebar showing git branches, PR status, listening ports, and intelligent notification rings.
