Based on the search results, I found substantial discussion about developers losing context in AI coding sessions. Here's what the discourse reveals:

## The Core Problem

AI coding agents forget project context because they operate on stateless inference with session-scoped memory, requiring developers to re-establish architecture, conventions, and dependencies at every session boundary. AI coding agents lose context because model weights are frozen, context windows rebuild from scratch, and most tools do not write cross-session memory by default.

## Real-World Impact

A 2025 randomized controlled study involving 16 experienced open-source developers completing 246 tasks across AI-assisted and control conditions found that developers using AI tools took 19% longer to complete tasks than those who did not. For a standard 2-hour focused development block, that overhead corresponds to roughly 20-25 minutes of additional time, reflecting total AI-related overhead including prompting, waiting for generations, and correcting context-misaligned output. For a team of 10 engineers across a two-week sprint, that overhead translates to roughly 2-3 full engineering days lost to AI-related friction.

## Developer Solutions

The community has developed various workarounds:

- Treating AI session state like code by versioning it and making it explicit
- Using structured handoff data including session status, key decisions, and TODOs, because AI models parse structured text much better than freeform notes
- Tools like tmux-assistant-resurrect, a tmux plugin that automatically saves and restores AI coding assistant sessions across tmux restarts
- cli-continues, which lets developers resume AI coding sessions across different CLI tools without losing context by seamlessly switching assistants while preserving conversation history, file edits, and workflow state

## The Deeper Issue

Context compaction erodes trust in AI coding tools. When an AI forgets your context, you start treating it differently. Context management is actually one of the highest-leverage unsolved problems in AI-assisted development, representing a fundamental shift in how developers can trust and rely on AI coding partners.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about Claude Code dashboard, session overview, and unified views for managing multiple sessions in 2026:

## Claude Code Agent View - Unified Session Dashboard

Claude Code Agent View is a research preview feature, launched May 11, 2026, that gives developers a unified CLI dashboard for managing multiple concurrent Claude Code sessions from a single screen. It is available on Pro, Max, Team, Enterprise, and Claude API plans, and requires Claude Code v2.1.139 or later.

## Key Features

Running `claude agents` opens a single list of every Claude Code session you have open, with each row showing the session, the last response, the timestamp, and whether the agent needs you.

Agent View natively knows the state of every session by reading directly from the state of the Claude Code process; features disk-backed persistence guaranteed by the supervisor process so sessions persist across terminal closures and reboots; and isolates writes onto separate worktrees, which avoids collisions when multiple agents touch the same files.

## Desktop and Native UI Components

The multi-session sidebar transforms the app into an orchestration dashboard and includes a new sidebar for managing multiple sessions, a drag-and-drop layout for arranging your workspace, an integrated terminal and file editor.

## Comparison with Codex

Codex's diff view features several more options, including the ability to switch between unified diff and split views, while Claude Code only offers a unified view. OpenAI's Codex Desktop app launched in February 2026 with a visual command center for parallel cloud tasks, and Agent View is Anthropic's answer from the terminal sideРђћthey solve the same problem with fundamentally different architectures.

```text
# Claude Code Agent View: Key Functions & Limitations

## Core Functionality

Agent View is a CLI dashboard launching with `claude agents` that displays all parallel Claude Code sessions in a single unified table. It addresses the fragmentation problem of managing multiple concurrent tasks across separate terminal windows.

## Attention & Prioritization

Yes, the system explicitly prioritizes sessions needing your input. "Sessions that need you, for example those blocked on a permission request or a multiple-choice question, automatically rise to the top of the list, above sessions that are working, above completed ones" according to the article.

Each session displays a colored icon indicating its stateРђћWorking, Needs input (yellow), Idle, Completed (green), Failed (red), or Stopped (gray)Рђћmaking status immediately visible.

## Cross-Vendor Compatibility

**No.** Agent View is exclusive to Claude Code. The article discusses comparisons with competing tools like tmux and mentions that "subagents are reusable agent configurations," but there's no indication it works with competing platforms like Codex or other vendors.

## State Persistence

Yes, Agent View stores task state durably. "The state of each session lives on disk under the Claude Code configuration directory" in JSON files. The supervisor process "automatically restarts when the Claude Code auto-updater installs a new binary version," and you can "close the laptop, open it ten minutes later and find every session exactly where you left it."

## Key Limitations

1. **Local-only:** Sessions stop if your computer sleeps or shuts down
2. **Quota burn:** Background sessions "consume quota exactly like interactive sessions," so ten parallel agents burn quota ten times faster
3. **Paid plans only:** Currently unavailable on free Claude accounts
4. **Experimental status:** Still in Research Preview as of May 2026

## Developer Workflow Telemetry & Behavioral Analysis

Telemetry can uncover interesting patterns in developers' behavior and serves as a behavioral lens on developer workflows. Researchers have analyzed what actually happens in code editors over extended periods: how much code is written, how often code is edited or undone, when external snippets are inserted, and how frequently developers switch back into the IDE from other tools.

## AI Code Detection & Analytics

Telemetry shows 22% of merged code can be AI-authored. Analytics compare AI-generated code outcomes to human-written code by measuring cycle times, rework rates, incident rates, and test coverage. Longitudinal tracking monitors AI-touched code over 30 or more days to reveal technical debt patterns and long-term quality impacts.

## Productivity Patterns

Research reveals AI coding assistants increase developer output, but not company productivity. While controlled trials highlight risks for experts on complex tasks, broader market telemetry from millions of pull requests shows substantial gains when including different types of work and teams.

## AI Observability Framework

AI observability provides insight into AI system behavior by gathering runtime data such as prompts, model responses, cost, and quality metrics, allowing teams to debug complex prompt chains and measure accuracy and hallucination rates.

66	    try {
67	      obj = JSON.parse(line) as JsonlEntry;

75	      session.lastTs = ts;

83	      session.actions += countActions(obj.message?.content);
84	    }
85	  }
86	  return session;

import { spawnCommand } from "../src/core/spawn.js";
import type { Brief } from "../src/core/types.js";

function brief(over: Partial<Brief>): Brief {
  return {
    path: "/v/p/brief.md",
    projectDir: "D:\\workspace\\proj",
    name: "proj",
    frontMatter: {},
    what: "",
    accept: "",
    next: "",
    status: "active",
    deferUntil: null,
    ...over,
  };
}

describe("spawnCommand", () => {
  it("emits a cd + vendor invocation with what and next", () => {
    const cmd = spawnCommand(brief({ what: "build it", next: "wire the API" }), "claude");
    expect(cmd).toContain('cd "D:\\workspace\\proj"');
    expect(cmd).toContain('claude "build it\\n\\nNext: wire the API"');
  });

  it("uses the codex binary for the codex vendor", () => {
    expect(spawnCommand(brief({ what: "x" }), "codex")).toContain('codex "x"');
  });

  it("escapes embedded quotes and backslashes", () => {

    expect(cmd).toContain('\\"hi\\"');
    expect(cmd).toContain("C:\\\\a");
  });
});

import fs from "node:fs";
import os from "node:os";
import path from "node:path";
import { afterAll, describe, expect, it } from "vitest";
import { loadMemoryKeywords } from "../src/core/memory.js";

const dir = fs.mkdtempSync(path.join(os.tmpdir(), "attend-mem-"));
afterAll(() => fs.rmSync(dir, { recursive: true, force: true }));

function tmp(name: string, content: string): string {
  const p = path.join(dir, name);
  fs.writeFileSync(p, content, "utf-8");
  return p;
}

describe("loadMemoryKeywords", () => {
  it("extracts CJK bigrams and latin words, dropping stopwords", () => {
    const f = tmp("a.md", "the project tauri Т│еТёЈтіЏ у«Ауљє\nwith webview");
    const kw = loadMemoryKeywords([f]);
    expect(kw).toContain("tauri");
    expect(kw).toContain("webview");
    expect(kw).toContain("Т│еТёЈтіЏ");
    expect(kw).toContain("у«Ауљє");
    expect(kw).not.toContain("the");
    expect(kw).not.toContain("with");
  });

  it("ignores short latin tokens", () => {
    expect(loadMemoryKeywords([tmp("b.md", "go ts api")])).not.toContain("go");
  });

  it("unions and dedupes across sources, tolerating missing files", () => {
    const f1 = tmp("c.md", "alpha beta");
    const kw = loadMemoryKeywords([f1, path.join(dir, "missing.md"), tmp("d.md", "beta gamma")]);
    expect(kw.filter((k) => k === "beta")).toHaveLength(1);
    expect(kw).toEqual(expect.arrayContaining(["alpha", "beta", "gamma"]));
  });
});

```

import fs from "node:fs";
import os from "node:os";
import path from "node:path";
import { afterEach, describe, expect, it } from "vitest";
import { resolveConfig } from "../src/config.js";

const ENV_KEYS = [
  "ATTEND_VAULTS",
  "ATTEND_PORT",
  "ATTEND_HOST",
  "ATTEND_CLAUDE_PROJECTS",
  "ATTEND_CODEX_SESSIONS",
];

afterEach(() => {
  for (const k of ENV_KEYS) delete process.env[k];
});

describe("resolveConfig precedence", () => {
  it("falls back to platform defaults (cwd vault, port 5050, localhost)", () => {
    const c = resolveConfig({ positionals: [] });
    expect(c.port).toBe(5050);
    expect(c.host).toBe("127.0.0.1");
    expect(c.vaultRoots).toEqual([path.resolve(process.cwd())]);
    expect(c.open).toBe(true);
  });

  it("uses positional dirs as vault roots over env/defaults", () => {
    process.env.ATTEND_VAULTS = "/should/be/ignored";
    const c = resolveConfig({ positionals: ["foo", "bar"] });
    expect(c.vaultRoots).toEqual([path.resolve("foo"), path.resolve("bar")]);
  });

  it("env beats config file; CLI beats env for port", () => {
    process.env.ATTEND_PORT = "6001";
    expect(resolveConfig({ positionals: [] }).port).toBe(6001);
    expect(resolveConfig({ positionals: [], port: "7002" }).port).toBe(7002);
  });

  it("reads a config file when given, but CLI/env still win", () => {
    const dir = fs.mkdtempSync(path.join(os.tmpdir(), "attend-cfg-"));
    const cfg = path.join(dir, "attend.config.json");
    fs.writeFileSync(cfg, JSON.stringify({ port: 8123, host: "0.0.0.0" }), "utf-8");
    try {
      const c = resolveConfig({ positionals: [], config: cfg });
      expect(c.port).toBe(8123);
      expect(c.host).toBe("0.0.0.0");
      expect(resolveConfig({ positionals: [], config: cfg, port: "9000" }).port).toBe(9000);
    } finally {
      fs.rmSync(dir, { recursive: true, force: true });
    }
  });

  it("--no-open disables auto-open", () => {
    expect(resolveConfig({ positionals: [], noOpen: true }).open).toBe(false);
  });
});

I have everything I need. Here is my decision-ready report.

---

# attend Рђћ PM-A Review (User-Workflow & Behavioral Lens)

**Reviewer:** PM-A ┬и **Date:** 2026-05-31 ┬и **Scope:** real pain points, competitive landscape, gap analysis, prioritized backlog. No code written.

---

## 1. The real pain of a solo dev running 10+ concurrent AI sessions

The design doc frames four costs (concurrency, vendor isolation, decision avoidance, polling overhead). After engaging with the behavioral literature it cites and the 2025РђЊ26 market, here's my refined read of what is *genuinely* painful vs. a non-problem.

**Genuinely painful (in priority order):**

1. **"Which of my N briefs deserves my next hour?" Рђћ the meta-decision.** This is the real leak, and it's the one the whole industry is *not* solving (everyone is building status boards; see ┬Д2). Gollwitzer's implementation-intentions research says the cost concentrates at the meta-decision ("what should I do?"), not the execution. With 10+ briefs, the dev re-runs that meta-decision *every time they go idle*, with decayed working memory of each. attend's `next`-line discipline + ranked feed targets this directly. This is attend's true reason to exist.

2. **Re-warming cost on resume, not tab count.** UC-Irvine's 23-min refocus figure is real, but with AI sessions the deeper cost is that *the human's* model of "where was this task" has decayed, AND the session's context window has accumulated dead context. The design's "kill cold sessions, resume from brief" instinct is correct and is **not** what competitors do Рђћ they obsessively try to keep worktree/session state alive (see Conductor, Crystal/Nimbalyst). attend is contrarian here in a defensible way.

3. **Avoidant procrastination is real and self-report can't see it (Steel 2007).** The dev who has a hard architectural decision open in session A will "go verify and push" easy session B and rationalize it as batching. This is the single most behaviorally interesting claim in the design, and the `avoidance` pattern (РЅЦ5 prompts, 0 actions) is a genuinely novel telemetry idea. *Nobody else is doing behavioral classification* Рђћ competitors only show running/blocked/ready *mechanical* status. **However**, see ┬Д3: the current trigger is too crude to be trustworthy.

4. **Review/verification fatigue.** The 2025РђЊ26 literature is now unanimous that the human reviewer is the bottleneck (Faros AI: +47% PRs/day with high AI adoption; "review fatigue" where deep evaluation degrades to surface scanning). This is a pain attend *touches* (the `active`/`healthy` patterns hint at it) but does not yet *address* Рђћ there's no "this brief has N unreviewed actions waiting on you" signal.

**Overstated / non-problems:**

- **Vendor isolation (cost #2).** The market has largely closed this gap since the design was written. Codex CLI now syncs CLIРєћDesktopРєћCloud off one backend; Claude Code, Codex, Gemini all land in vibe-kanban / Claude Squad / cmux uniformly. "Manual copy-paste is the current state" is increasingly *not* true. Vendor-neutrality is still a sound *architectural* principle (invariant 4) but it is weak as a *user-facing differentiator* in 2026.
- **Pre-crastination as a distinct signal.** Rosenbaum's distinction (easy-task-first to free working memory is fine if it ends in 1РђЊ2h) is intellectually elegant but attend has no way to measure "did the easy task end in 1РђЊ2h" without time-as-input, which the design rejects. This is currently a touchstone with no implementation hook Рђћ fine as philosophy, not actionable.

---

## 2. Competitive / community landscape (2025РђЊ26)

The field has exploded and converged on **one pattern attend deliberately does not occupy**. Mapping it:

### A. Worktree-per-agent orchestrators (the dominant cluster)
- **vibe-kanban** (BloopAI, ~9.4kРўЁ, Rust+React, kanban board, 10+ agents, isolated git worktree per task; now sunsetting commercially Рєњ community OSS). [vibekanban.com](https://vibekanban.com/), [GitHub](https://github.com/langchain-ai/agent-inbox)
- **Conductor** (free Mac app, parallel Claude Code sessions, worktree + setup script + auto-branch + `/resolve-merge-conflicts`). [conductor.build via George Taskos](https://georgetaskos.medium.com/scaling-the-loop-run-5-claude-code-sessions-in-parallel-with-conductor-build-539b52888a81)
- **Crystal Рєњ Nimbalyst** (desktop app, parallel Codex + Claude worktrees; Crystal deprecated Feb 2026). [GitHub](https://github.com/stravu/crystal), [nimbalyst.com](https://nimbalyst.com/blog/crystal-supercharge-your-development-with-multi-session-claude-code-management/)
- **Claude Squad** (TUI, tmux + worktrees, multi-vendor, background/yolo mode). [GitHub](https://github.com/smtg-ai/claude-squad)

**What they get right:** isolation (worktree) genuinely prevents parallel agents clobbering each other Рђћ the #1 *mechanical* pain.
**What they get wrong / what attend should avoid:** they all (a) **auto-spawn and orchestrate** Рєњ recreate the N-tab cascade attend explicitly rejects; (b) are **per-session, not per-task** Рђћ when the session dies, so does the unit of management (violates attend's brief=state insight); (c) optimize *throughput* (run more agents) not *attention allocation* (which one should I, the human, engage). They make you a faster orchestrator of a bigger queue; attend tries to make you *open fewer of them, deliberately*. attend is right to NOT compete here.

### B. Status-board / command-center cluster
- **Windsurf 2.0 Agent Command Center** Рђћ kanban grouped by Running/Blocked/Ready, local+cloud agents. [docs.windsurf.com](https://docs.windsurf.com/windsurf/agent-command-center)
- **Cursor 2.x** Рђћ up to 8 parallel agent sessions, background-agent status text, `/loop`. [cursor.com/changelog](https://cursor.com/changelog)
- **cmux** (Manaflow, ~7.7kРўЁ, Ghostty-based macOS terminal) Рђћ sidebar with git/PR/port status, **green/yellow/red notification rings** (done / waiting-for-input / error). [explainx.ai](https://www.explainx.ai/blog/cmux-terminal-ai-coding-agents-2026)
- **Sculptor** (Imbue) Рђћ Docker-isolated agents, bidirectional Pairing Mode. [augmentcode.com](https://www.augmentcode.com/tools/best-ai-coding-agent-desktop-apps)

**What they get right:** at-a-glance "who needs me" via color/status (cmux's rings are the gold standard of the *mechanical* version of attend's pattern classifier).
**What they get wrong:** status is **mechanical** (process running? blocked on input?), never **behavioral/semantic** ("are you avoiding this?" / "does this align with what you care about?"). And they are **push-y** (notification rings) Рђћ the opposite of attend's pull invariant. Lesson: attend's pattern badges are its cmux-rings analog but with a behavioral instead of process-state semantics Рђћ that's the differentiation to sharpen, NOT abandon.

### C. Human-in-the-loop inbox
- **LangChain Agent Inbox** Рђћ inbox UX for HITL interrupts (accept/edit/respond/ignore). [GitHub](https://github.com/langchain-ai/agent-inbox)

This is the closest thing to a "single surface for many agents," and DESIGN.md already rejected it (push, and diagnostic cost is near-zero when polling = idle). That rejection still holds Рђћ Agent Inbox assumes *agents interrupt you*; attend assumes *you decide when to look*. Correct call, no change.

### D. Knowledge / context-persistence cluster
- **Karpathy LLM Wiki** (Apr 2026, 5kРўЁ in 2 weeks): raw-sources / LLM-owned wiki / schema-file (CLAUDE.md). A community v2 adds a **"hot cache" layer updated at end of every session** so the next resumes with no recap. [gist](https://gist.github.com/karpathy/442a6bf555914893e9891c11519de94f), [v2 gist](https://gist.github.com/rohitg00/2067ab416f7bbe447c1977edaaa681e2)
- **Anthropic context-editing + memory tools** (Sept 2025): memory+context-editing = +39% agent perf. **Git-Context-Controller (GCC)**: COMMIT/BRANCH/MERGE for agent memory. [thenewstack.io](https://thenewstack.io/context-is-ai-codings-real-bottleneck-in-2026/)

**Big strategic signal for attend:** the brief's `next` line **is** the hot.md/hot-cache pattern, validated independently and going viral. This is attend's strongest tailwind Рђћ but also a risk: DESIGN.md's "rejected paths" notes that base models + memory tools may deliver PKM upstream. The hot-cache pattern landing *inside Claude Code's own memory* is exactly that upstream encroachment. attend should lean into being the **cross-task, cross-vendor index over many hot.md briefs** Рђћ the one thing per-session memory can't be.

### Net competitive position
attend is the **only** tool optimizing *human attention allocation across durable tasks*, while everyone else optimizes *agent throughput across transient sessions*. That is a real, unoccupied niche. The risk is not a competitor Рђћ it's that the niche is *narrow* and the current telemetry isn't yet trustworthy enough to make the dev open the page daily (┬Д3).

---

## 3. Gap analysis Рђћ is the telemetry/pattern/priority model useful or vanity?

**Verdict: the model is conceptually the best in the market but currently sits on the wrong side of the trust line. It is closer to "interesting" than "I open this every morning."** Specifics:

**Telemetry Рђћ under-discriminating.**
- `actions = Edit/Write/Bash count` conflates "productive code change" with "ran `ls` 30 times." A high action count can be thrash, not flow. The `healthy` definition (`actions>0, dwellРЅЦ10min`) can therefore mislabel flailing as flow. **This is the trust-killer.**
- `avoidance` = "РЅЦ5 prompts, 0 actions." This fires on *legitimate* read-only/planning sessions (architecture discussion, debugging-by-reading) just as hard as on true avoidance. A solo dev who hits one false "avoidance" label on a session they know was productive thinking will distrust every badge thereafter. The behavioral idea is gold; the operationalization is too blunt.
- **No "unreviewed work waiting on you" signal** Рђћ the #1 industry pain (┬Д1.4) is invisible to attend.

**Pattern classifier Рђћ sound philosophy, brittle thresholds.** The descriptive-not-judgmental constraint (invariant 3) is genuinely well-executed and differentiates from cmux's red-error framing. But thresholds (5 prompts, 7 days, 10 min) are untuned magic numbers with no per-user calibration and no confidence/uncertainty surfaced. A pattern shown as fact when it's a low-confidence guess violates the *spirit* of "trustworthy."

**Priority evaluator Рђћ the keystone, and the weakest link.**
- Priority = `memory-alignment├Ќ2 + pattern + blocker Рѕњ defer`. **Memory-alignment is doing the heavy lifting and is the least robust part**: keyword-hit counting against MEMORY.md keywords is a bag-of-words proxy for "semantic alignment with what I care about." It will rank a brief high because it shares the word "auth" with memory, regardless of actual relevance. This is the difference between a useful morning ranking and a vanity ranking. The design *knows* this (v1+ LLM priority is roadmapped, env-flagged) Рђћ but until then the headline feature (ranking) rests on the shakiest heuristic.
- The **one-line reason per rank is the single best design decision** Рђћ it makes the score auditable and overridable, which is exactly what lets a skeptical dev trust *or correct* it. Keep and strengthen this.

**The "open it every morning" test.** Today the page answers "here are your briefs, ranked by a keyword heuristic, with behavioral badges that fire on crude thresholds." For a daily habit it needs to answer, credibly: **"Here is the ONE thing to do next, here's exactly why, and here's what's quietly rotting."** That requires (a) trustworthy avoidance/stalled detection, (b) better-than-keyword alignment, and (c) a surfacing of decay/staleness that doesn't rely on the rejected hot/warm/cold-by-time model. The good news: all three are reachable without touching invariants.

---

## 4. Prioritized recommendation backlog

Effort: S = <1 day, M = ~2РђЊ4 days, L = ~1 week+. Every item annotated against the 5 invariants and the rejected-paths list.

### MUST-DO (makes it trustworthy enough for a daily habit)

| # | Recommendation | Pain addressed | Effort | Invariant check |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| M1 | **Make `avoidance` defensible: add confidence + escape hatches.** Distinguish read/plan-heavy sessions from true avoidance (e.g. require РЅЦ5 prompts AND 0 actions AND span >1РђЊ2h Рђћ directly operationalizing Rosenbaum's "did the easy task end in 1РђЊ2h" test, which is currently philosophy-only). Surface as "looks likeРђд" with a visible confidence, not a verdict. | False "avoidance" labels destroy trust in all badges (┬Д3) | M | РюЁ Respects inv 3 (still descriptive; *more* so). Note: uses session *duration*, not time-of-day/recency Рђћ does NOT reintroduce the rejected "hot/warm/cold by time" model (that ranked by recency; this measures task-internal dwell, already a v0 signal). |
| M2 | **Replace keyword memory-alignment with embedding similarity (still local, no LLM call needed for v1).** Brief text Рєћ memory text cosine similarity beats bag-of-words and needs no API. Keep the one-line reason ("aligns with memory: <top matched concept>"). | The headline ranking currently rests on the weakest heuristic (┬Д3) | M | РюЁ All invariants. Local embeddings keep it offline; doesn't require the env-flagged LLM path, so no new dependency on vendor execution (inv 4 fine). |
| M3 | **Surface "unreviewed actions waiting on you" per brief.** Count actions since the brief's `last_touch` / since the human last engaged Рђћ the review-fatigue signal the whole market has (┬Д1.4) and attend is blind to. | #1 industry-wide pain; currently invisible | SРђЊM | РюЁ Pull, descriptive, derived from existing JSONL. Fully invariant-safe. |
| M4 | **Get real data in the feed (brief skeletons for live projects) + `attend new`.** The feed is unproven on real repos; an empty/thin feed fails the morning test on day one. | Validation gap; cwd-matching unproven (brief.md `next` option A) | S | РюЁ Pure scaffolding. Invariant-safe. |

### SHOULD-DO (deepens the moat in the unoccupied niche)

| # | Recommendation | Pain addressed | Effort | Invariant check |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| S1 | **`attend split` CLI.** Read JSONL Рєњ LLM proposes N candidate briefs Рєњ **user gates selection** (gate is non-optional, per brief.md). | "session keeps diverging" Рєњ externalize to durable briefs; this is the highest-ROI roadmap item | M | РюЁ Inv 1 (extracts state to briefs), inv 4 (reads neutral JSONL). Explicitly NOT auto-spawn (rejected path respected). |
| S2 | **Codex JSONL parser** (the `SessionSource` stub). | Vendor-neutrality is currently aspirational; only Claude is parsed | M | РюЁ Inv 4 by construction. |
| S3 | **Per-user threshold calibration** (let the dev's own history set what "long dwell" / "many prompts" means instead of fixed 5/7/10). | Magic-number thresholds erode trust (┬Д3) | M | РюЁ Inv 3 (still descriptive). Uses dwell distribution, not recency Рєњ not the rejected time model. |
| S4 | **"Quietly rotting" surfacing for `stalled`/`deferred-with-met-condition` briefs**, framed as observation ("3 briefs deferred-until conditions that now appear met"). Note: the abandoned-side-project literature (┬Д research) says *deliberate* killing is healthy Рђћ so frame as "review for kill," never "you abandoned this." | Unintentional abandonment is the real graveyard pain; supports deliberate-quit | S | РюЁ Inv 3 critically Рђћ wording must stay non-judgmental. |

### NICE-TO-HAVE

| # | Recommendation | Pain | Effort | Invariant check |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| N1 | Dwell-distribution heatmap per brief (roadmap item). | Texture for flow/thrash discrimination | M | РюЁ |
| N2 | In-browser brief edit (roadmap). | Friction; FS-only editing | M | РюЁ Рђћ but keep file as source of truth (inv 1). |
| N3 | LLM-based priority (env-flagged, roadmap). | Best-quality alignment | M | РюЁ as specified (env-flag, optional). |

### FLAGGED Рђћ violates an invariant or rejected path (argue explicitly, high bar)

- **Optional, opt-in single "needs-you" digest (e.g. one local desktop ping when РЅЦ1 brief crosses a high-confidence avoidance/stall threshold).** *Violates inv 2 (pull-not-push) and matches the rejected "push notifications / Agent Inbox" path.* **Argument to overturn:** the original rejection rested on "polling = idle moments, so push can't help (can't act when not idle)." That premise is about *interrupting work*. A **once-daily, opt-in, pull-shaped digest the user requests** (cron they own, not vendor-driven interrupts) is closer to "scheduled pull" than "push." **My recommendation: do NOT build it.** The bar is high and the premise still mostly holds for a solo dev; cmux/Windsurf already prove push-rings create exactly the noise attend exists to escape. I flag it only to document that I considered and rejected it Рђћ it stays rejected.

- **Worktree/spawn orchestration (├а la vibe-kanban/Conductor).** *Violates the auto-spawn rejected path + the whole "reduce orchestration load" thesis.* **Do not build.** This is the crowded commodity; entering it abandons the differentiation. Keep spawn = copy-to-clipboard.

---

## 5. Highest-conviction recommendation

**Fix trust before adding surface area: ship M1 + M2 together (defensible avoidance detection + embedding-based memory alignment, with the existing one-line reasons).**

attend's entire thesis is that it can tell a solo dev *the one task to engage next and what they're quietly avoiding* Рђћ a niche literally no competitor occupies (everyone else builds throughput orchestrators and process-status boards). But that thesis lives or dies on whether the dev *believes the page*. Right now the two signals carrying the whole product Рђћ the `avoidance` badge and the memory-alignment ranking Рђћ are the two crudest heuristics in the codebase (bag-of-words and a single blunt prompt-count threshold). One false "avoidance" call or one obviously-wrong #1 ranking and the dev never opens the page again. Every other backlog item (`split`, Codex, heatmaps) adds reach to a foundation that isn't yet load-bearing. Make the existing signals *trustworthy and auditable* first Рђћ that, not more features, is what earns the every-morning open. Both fit cleanly inside all five invariants and require no push, no orchestration, no second surface.

---

```typescript
import { describe, expect, it } from "vitest";
import { parseClaudeTranscript } from "../src/core/vendor/claude.js";

function jsonl(...lines: object[]): string {
  return lines.map((l) => JSON.stringify(l)).join("\n");
}

describe("parseClaudeTranscript", () => {
  it("counts user text prompts and assistant action tool uses; captures cwd + ts span", () => {
    const raw = jsonl(
      { type: "user", cwd: "D:\\proj", timestamp: "2026-05-01T10:00:00Z", message: { content: "do X" } },
      {
        type: "assistant",
        timestamp: "2026-05-01T10:05:00Z",
        message: { content: [{ type: "tool_use", name: "Edit" }, { type: "tool_use", name: "Bash" }] },
      },
      { type: "user", timestamp: "2026-05-01T10:10:00Z", message: { content: "do Y" } },
    );
    const s = parseClaudeTranscript("s.jsonl", raw);
    expect(s.cwd).toBe("D:\\proj");
    expect(s.prompts).toBe(2);
    expect(s.actions).toBe(2);
    expect(s.firstTs).toBe(Date.parse("2026-05-01T10:00:00Z"));
    expect(s.lastTs).toBe(Date.parse("2026-05-01T10:10:00Z"));
  });

  it("ignores command-like prompts (content starting with '<') and non-action tools", () => {
    const raw = jsonl(
      { type: "user", message: { content: "<command-name>clear</command-name>" } },
      { type: "assistant", message: { content: [{ type: "tool_use", name: "Read" }] } },
    );
    const s = parseClaudeTranscript("s.jsonl", raw);
    expect(s.prompts).toBe(0);
    expect(s.actions).toBe(0);
  });

  it("does not count subagent sidechain turns toward prompts/actions", () => {
    const raw = jsonl(
      { type: "user", isSidechain: true, message: { content: "subagent task" } },
      { type: "assistant", isSidechain: true, message: { content: [{ type: "tool_use", name: "Write" }] } },
      { type: "user", message: { content: "real prompt" } },
    );
    const s = parseClaudeTranscript("s.jsonl", raw);
    expect(s.prompts).toBe(1);
    expect(s.actions).toBe(0);
  });

  it("tolerates malformed lines and non-transcript record types", () => {
    const raw = ["not json", JSON.stringify({ type: "summary" }), ""].join("\n");
    const s = parseClaudeTranscript("s.jsonl", raw);
    expect(s.prompts).toBe(0);
    expect(s.cwd).toBeNull();
  });
});

```

---

# attend Рђћ Strategy / Differentiation / Scope Review (PM-B)

## TL;DR for the decision-maker

The market moved *hard* into attend's category while it was being built Рђћ but into the part attend deliberately rejected (orchestration/spawn/worktree/kanban), and **the platform itself (Claude Code "Agent View", shipped May 11 2026) now owns the naive version of attend's single-surface "needs-you" dashboard**. That is simultaneously the biggest threat and the clarifying gift: it commoditizes the *session-status* layer and leaves attend's actually-defensible wedge exposed and unclaimed. attend should retreat *up* a level Рђћ to **cross-vendor, task-durable, behaviorally-aware attention routing** Рђћ and kill everything that drifts toward being a worse Conductor.

---

## 1. The Wedge

### What attend should be unambiguously best at
> **"The one place that tells a solo dev *which task to re-engage next* Рђћ across Claude Code AND Codex Рђћ using durable per-task state and behavioral signals the vendors don't compute."**

Three load-bearing words, in priority order of defensibility:

1. **Behavioral** Рђћ the avoidance/stalled/healthy classifier grounded in Steel/Gollwitzer. **Nobody else is doing this.** Every "telemetry" product in the AI-coding space measures code/org outcomes (rework rate, incident rate, AI-authored %) Рђћ see [JetBrains research](https://blog.jetbrains.com/research/2026/04/ai-impact-developer-workflows/) and [Faros](https://www.faros.ai/blog/ai-software-engineering). None classify *the operator's* behavior to surface decision-avoidance. This is true whitespace and the hardest thing to copy because it requires a *point of view* (the Steel constraint), not just a parser.
2. **Cross-vendor** Рђћ Claude + Codex in one view. The native dashboards are explicitly single-vendor (confirmed below). Real defensibility, but a *thin* moat: it's "a second JSONL parser," and it evaporates if a third party ships a good cross-vendor view first (Claude Squad/Conductor already launch *both* vendors, just not in a unified ranked feed yet).
3. **Task-durable (brief = state)** Рђћ survives session death; resume-from-brief instead of resume-cold-session. Strong conceptually and validated by the whole SDD wave, but **muddiest in positioning** (see below).

### Is the current positioning coherent and defensible?
**Coherent: mostly. Defensible: only if it stops describing itself as a "router/dashboard"** Рђћ because that noun now belongs to the platform.

Where it's muddy:

- **"attention router" collides head-on with Claude Code Agent View.** As of May 11 2026, `claude agents` shows every session in one list, and *"sessions that need you Рђд automatically rise to the top, above sessions that are working, above completed ones,"* with disk-backed durable state surviving reboots ([source](https://pasqualepillitteri.it/en/news/2384/claude-code-agent-view-cli-dashboard-sessions-2026)). That is attend's invariant-5 "single polling surface" + a crude priority sort + invariant-1 durability Рђћ shipped natively, for one vendor. If attend keeps marketing "single localhost page replacing N tabs," a user's reasonable response is *"Agent View already does that for free."* The "router" framing is now a commodity.
- **"brief = durable state" collides with Spec-Driven Development.** SDD is the dominant 2026 discourse Рђћ *"specs are durable docs that outlive the code Рђд future agents and humans read them as the canonical reference"* ([BCMS](https://thebcms.com/blog/spec-driven-development), [Augment](https://www.augmentcode.com/guides/what-is-spec-driven-development)). attend's brief reads like an SDD spec but is a *different artifact*: a spec is **upstream-of-execution** ("build this"), a brief is **attention/resume state** ("here's where I'm stuck, what to try next"). The product never disambiguates this, so a knowledgeable user pattern-matches briefРєњspec and asks "why not just use Spec Kit / OpenSpec?" The `next` line (stuck-state, Gollwitzer implementation-intention) is the actual differentiator and it's buried.
- **The pull-only / behavioral / single-user stance is the moat but reads as a list of *omissions*.** The README's "What this is NOT" is longer and louder than "what it uniquely is." Strategically the negatives are correct; rhetorically they make attend sound like a deliberately-crippled Conductor rather than a different category.

**Verdict:** The bet is *coherent* but the *headline noun is now commoditized*. Re-anchor the wedge on **behavioral routing of durable tasks across vendors** Рђћ the one combination no incumbent or platform holds.

---

## 2. Market / Community: how devs orchestrate many agents in 2025РђЊ26

### The landscape splits into four camps

| Camp | Representative tools | Shape | Relation to attend |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Worktree parallelizers** | [parallel-code](https://github.com/johannesjo/parallel-code), [ccswarm](https://github.com/nwiizo/ccswarm), [agent-orchestrator](https://github.com/ComposioHQ/agent-orchestrator), Superset | Spawn N agents, isolate via git worktree, one branch/PR each | **Rejected path** (auto-spawn cascade). Not competitors Рђћ adjacent. |
| **Visual orchestrators / kanban** | [Conductor](https://madewithlove.com/blog/conductor-running-multiple-ai-coding-agents-in-parallel/), Crystal, Vibe Kanban (bloop shut down Apr 2026, now community OSS), [Nimbalyst](https://nimbalyst.com/blog/best-multi-agent-coding-tools-2026/) | Dashboard + diff-review + notifications, spawn-centric | **Closest competitors.** Diff-first, *session*-centric, push-notify. |
| **TUI session managers** | [Claude Squad](https://vibecodinghub.org/tools/claude-squad) | One TUI, isolated workspace per agent, multi-vendor launch | Cross-vendor (good!) but TUI = attend's rejected form. |
| **Async / cloud fire-and-forget** | Codex cloud/Desktop, Claude Code async subagents | Describe task Рєњ cloud container Рєњ collect diff later | The "fewer-longer-lived" trend. Orthogonal to local-session sprawl. |

### The trend line Рђћ and the answer to "is many-sessions-+-briefs the right bet?"

There are **two trends, not one**, and they pull in opposite directions:

- **Trend A Рђћ orchestration consolidation:** "the human is the bottleneck, not the model" is now consensus ([New Stack](https://thenewstack.io/context-is-ai-codings-real-bottleneck-in-2026/), [dev.to: code review is the real bottleneck](https://dev.to/code-board/code-review-is-the-real-bottleneck-of-2026-and-most-teams-dont-see-it-5eed)). The market's answer is *unified dashboards + diff-review + "needs-attention" notifications* (Superset, Conductor, Agent View). This **validates attend's core premise** (attention is the scarce resource) but converges on a UX attend explicitly refuses (push, diff-review, spawn).
- **Trend B Рђћ fewer, longer-lived, async agents:** 2026 guidance is "start narrow, async workflows, avoid unnecessary orchestration/memory overhead" ([Fluence](https://www.fluence.network/blog/ai-agent-examples/), [bits-bytes](https://bits-bytes-nn.github.io/insights/agentic-ai/2026/04/05/evolution-of-ai-agentic-patterns-en.html)). Codex cloud + Claude async subagents push toward *delegate-and-collect*, which **reduces** the count of *human-attended* live sessions.

**Implication for the bet:** attend's framing Рђћ *"10+ concurrent live sessions is normal and not the goal to reduce"* (DESIGN ┬ДProblem) Рђћ is **partially eroding**. Async/cloud delegation is genuinely lowering the number of sessions a human babysits at once. BUT the durable-state premise gets *stronger*, not weaker: whether you run 10 live or 3 async, the cross-vendor, cross-session-death **task** is the stable unit, and the SDD wave + the "never lose context between sessions" discourse ([multiple dev.to posts](https://dev.to/gonewx/the-hidden-cost-of-ai-coding-context-loss-and-how-developers-are-fixing-it-4b0d)) prove the market feels this pain acutely. **"Many sessions" is a softening bet; "durable cross-vendor task state + behavioral routing" is a strengthening bet.** Re-weight the pitch accordingly.

### The platform-encroachment alarm (most important market fact)
**Claude Code Agent View (May 11 2026) is attend's single-surface "needs-you" dashboard, shipped by the platform owner.** It is local, disk-persistent, ranks "needs-you" to the top, and isolates worktrees. It is **vendor-locked (no Codex), session-centric (not task/brief-centric), and computes no behavioral classification** Рђћ but it commoditizes the part of attend that's easiest to demo. Codex Desktop shipped its own "visual command center for parallel cloud tasks" (Feb 2026). **Both vendors now own the in-vendor version of attend's surface.** attend's only durable ground is the union: *cross-vendor + task-durable + behavioral*, which neither platform will build (no incentive to be vendor-neutral, no Steel-style POV).

---

## 3. MVP Рєњ Great: roadmap triage

Current state: parity with the Python prototype, ported to Node/TS + tests + `npx` packaging. v1 roadmap from DESIGN.md, scored by leverage:

| Roadmap item | Leverage | Why | Invariant check |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Codex JSONL parser** | **HIGH** | This *is* the cross-vendor wedge. Today attend is a single-vendor dashboard competing with a *free, better* single-vendor dashboard (Agent View). Without Codex, attend has **no reason to exist over Agent View.** Highest-leverage item on the list. | Core to invariant 4. РюЁ |
| **`attend split`** (transcript Рєњ N candidate briefs) | **MEDIUM-HIGH** | Directly attacks "session sprawls into divergent threads" Рђћ a real, cited pain. It's also the one *generative* feature that's clearly *not* an SDD/orchestrator me-too. Risk: scope creep into an LLM product. Keep it candidate-only, user-gated (DESIGN already insists). | LLM dep + needs env-flag; respects invariant 1 (output = briefs = state). РюЁ if gated. |
| **`attend new` scaffold** | **MEDIUM** | Cheap, removes adoption friction (empty feed = dead product). But it's plumbing, not differentiation. Do it because it's a 1-hour job that makes everything else demoable, not because it's strategic. | РюЁ trivial. |
| **Blocker regex tightening** (`уГЅ[тєЁт«╣]` not bare `уГЅ`) | **MEDIUM** | Already fixed in `priority.ts` per the v1 pivot note. Correctness of the *routing signal* = core. Confirm it's done; if so, **strike from roadmap.** | РюЁ |
| **LLM-based priority (env-flag)** | **LOW-MEDIUM** | Tempting but dangerous. The heuristic's *legibility* ("one-line reason, no opaque scores," DESIGN ┬Д3) is a feature, not a limitation Рђћ it's what lets the user override. An LLM ranker risks trading the legible moat for an opaque score that competes withРђд every LLM-everything product. Defer until the heuristic demonstrably fails on real data. | Borderline. Opaque scoring tension with the "must be able to override" design principle. Рџа№ИЈ |
| **Dwell-distribution heatmap** | **LOW Рђћ CUT** | This is analytics-dashboard gravity. A per-brief heatmap is *judgmental-adjacent* (visualizing "time spent" invites the user to read it as performance), brushing invariant 3, and adds a second visual language to a page whose whole virtue is one glance Рєњ one decision. Pure feature-creep. | Рџа№ИЈ Risk to invariant 3 (judgmental drift) + invariant 5 (more to scan). **Kill.** |
| **In-browser brief edit** | **LOW Рђћ CUT (for now)** | Briefs are markdown found by `ls`, colocated with work; the user already lives in an editor. An in-browser editor (a) duplicates the IDE, (b) risks attend becoming the *write* surface, which pulls toward invariant-1 violation (state authored in the dashboard instead of the vault) and toward "second app." The dashboard should stay **view-only over an authoritative vault** (DESIGN ┬ДProduct form). | Рџа№ИЈ Erodes "daemon is view-only; vault is authoritative." **Kill or defer hard.** |

### What should be killed
- **Dwell heatmap** Рђћ analytics theater, invariant-3 drift. Kill.
- **In-browser edit** Рђћ pulls attend toward being the write-surface / second app. Kill (or defer behind a very high bar).
- **LLM priority** Рђћ defer until the legible heuristic is *proven* insufficient on real briefs; don't trade legibility for a score.

### What's missing from the roadmap but is high-leverage
1. **A crisp "vs. Agent View" answer baked into the product's first screen** Рђћ i.e., make the *task* (brief) the primary object and *sessions* secondary/derived, so the difference from session-centric Agent View is structural, not rhetorical. This is positioning-as-product, near-zero code.
2. **Eat your own dogfood signal loop** Рђћ `next` step **A** in brief.md (seed real briefs for `СйютЊЂB`/`lifeset`/`mcp`) is *correctly* identified as the highest-ROI next action: an empty feed can't validate the wedge. This precedes everything except Codex parsing.

---

## 4. Prioritized backlog (decision-ready)

### MUST
- **M1 Рђћ Seed real briefs on live projects + validate cwd matching** (brief.md `next` option A). *Rationale:* zero signal without data; de-risks the whole telemetry/priority chain on real input. *Effort: S.* *Invariants: none touched.* РюЁ
- **M2 Рђћ Codex JSONL parser** (fill the `SessionSource` stub). *Rationale:* the cross-vendor wedge is attend's only defensible ground vs. native Agent View / Codex Desktop. Without it attend is a strictly-worse single-vendor dashboard. *Effort: M* (needs a real Codex sample Рђћ currently blocked on the user enabling Codex CLI; unblock this first). *Invariants: core to #4.* РюЁ
- **M3 Рђћ Re-anchor positioning: task-first, "behavioral router," disambiguate from spec/Agent View** (docs + UI emphasis, not new subsystems). *Rationale:* the headline noun ("dashboard/router") is now commoditized; the survivable claim is "cross-vendor + behavioral + task-durable." *Effort: S.* *Invariants: reinforces all five.* РюЁ

### SHOULD
- **S1 Рђћ `attend split` (candidate briefs, user-gated)**. *Rationale:* the one generative feature that's differentiated (not SDD/orchestrator me-too) and attacks a cited pain. *Effort: M.* *Invariants: keep output = briefs (state), keep the human gate; env-flag the LLM. Borderline on "no LLM-product creep" Рђћ hold the line on candidates-not-answers.* РюЁ if disciplined.
- **S2 Рђћ `attend new` scaffold**. *Rationale:* removes the empty-feed adoption cliff; cheap. *Effort: S.* *Invariants: none.* РюЁ
- **S3 Рђћ Confirm/close blocker-regex fix**. *Rationale:* routing-signal correctness. Likely already done Рђћ verify and strike. *Effort: XS.* РюЁ

### NICE
- **N1 Рђћ LLM-based priority, env-flagged, OFF by default, heuristic remains primary**. *Rationale:* only if real data shows the heuristic mis-ranks; never replace the legible one-line reason. *Effort: M.* *Invariants: Рџа№ИЈ legibility/override tension Рђћ guard carefully.*

### KILL
- **K1 Рђћ Dwell heatmap** (Рџа№ИЈ invariant 3 judgmental-drift + invariant 5 scan-load; analytics theater).
- **K2 Рђћ In-browser brief edit** (Рџа№ИЈ invariant 1: pulls state-authoring into the dashboard; pushes toward second-app).

### Should an invariant be revisited?
**Invariant: "don't cap concurrent sessions / open РЅа active."** I'd *partially revisit the framing, not the rule.* The async/cloud-delegation trend (Codex cloud, Claude async subagents) is genuinely shrinking the count of *human-attended live sessions*. The invariant's instinct (durability over tab-reduction) is right, but the supporting narrative ("10+ live sessions is normal") is aging. Recommend: keep the rule, **re-cast the premise around "tasks outlive sessions whether you run 1 or 10"** Рђћ which is *more* defensible in 2026, not less. No code change; it's a positioning correction that future-proofs the pitch against the "fewer agents" trend.

All five invariants otherwise hold and are, if anything, *more* differentiating now that the platform has commoditized the surface they constrain. **Pull-only + descriptive-telemetry + single-surface are exactly the choices Conductor/Crystal/Agent View did NOT make** (they're push + diff-review + session-centric) Рђћ so the invariants are the brand. Don't soften them to chase parity.

---

## Single highest-conviction recommendation
**Ship the Codex parser and make the brief (task) the primary object Рђћ before anything else.** The day Claude Code Agent View shipped (May 11 2026), attend's single-vendor session dashboard became a strictly-worse free feature. attend's *entire* remaining reason to exist is the union the platforms structurally won't build: **cross-vendor + task-durable + behavioral routing.** Cross-vendor (Codex) is the load-bearing leg of that union and it's currently a stub. Everything else is secondary to closing it.

## Single strongest "do NOT build this" warning
**Do NOT build the dwell heatmap (and resist all analytics-dashboard gravity).** It violates the spirit of invariant 3 Рђћ visualizing "time spent per task" *is* judgmental feedback no matter how neutrally you label it, and Steel 2007 says that worsens the exact avoidance behavior attend exists to surface gently. It also adds a second thing to scan on a page whose only virtue is one-glance-one-decision (invariant 5). It would convert attend from a *decision aid* into a *productivity-surveillance dashboard* Рђћ the wrong category, competing with everyone, defensible against no one.

```typescript
/**
 * Memory alignment via TF-IDF cosine similarity. Replaces the original
 * bag-of-words keyword-hit count (PM-A: the headline ranking rested on the
 * weakest heuristic). Pure, local, no model download, no API call Рђћ keeps the
 * ranking legible (we report the top contributing terms as the reason).
 */

const STOPWORDS = new Set([
  "the", "and", "with", "this", "that", "from", "have", "will",
  "name", "description", "metadata", "type", "memory", "file",
  "your", "you", "for", "are", "but", "not", "they", "their",
]);

const LATIN = /[A-Za-z][A-Za-z0-9_-]{3,}/g;
const CJK_RUN = /[СИђ-ж┐┐]{2,}/g;

/**
 * Tokenize into a bag of terms (with repetition, for term frequency).
 * Latin words are lowercased; CJK runs are exploded into character bigrams so
 * "Т│еТёЈтіЏу«Ауљє" aligns with a memory mentioning "Т│еТёЈтіЏ" (shared Т│еТёЈ / ТёЈтіЏ).
 */
export function tokenize(text: string): string[] {
  const tokens: string[] = [];
  for (const w of text.match(LATIN) ?? []) {
    const lw = w.toLowerCase();
    if (!STOPWORDS.has(lw)) tokens.push(lw);
  }
  for (const run of text.match(CJK_RUN) ?? []) {
    for (let i = 0; i < run.length - 1; i++) tokens.push(run.slice(i, i + 2));
  }
  return tokens;
}

function termFreq(tokens: string[]): Map<string, number> {
  const tf = new Map<string, number>();
  for (const t of tokens) tf.set(t, (tf.get(t) ?? 0) + 1);
  return tf;
}

export interface AlignmentModel {
  /** tf-idf weighted profile vector of the whole memory corpus */
  profile: Map<string, number>;
  profileNorm: number;
  idf: Map<string, number>;
  vocabSize: number;
}

const EMPTY_MODEL: AlignmentModel = {
  profile: new Map(),
  profileNorm: 0,
  idf: new Map(),
  vocabSize: 0,
};

/**
 * Build an alignment model from memory documents (one string per memory file).
 * IDF uses sklearn-style smoothing so the model is well-defined even for a
 * single document.
 */
export function buildAlignmentModel(docs: string[]): AlignmentModel {
  const docTokens = docs.map(tokenize).filter((t) => t.length > 0);
  const n = docTokens.length;
  if (n === 0) return EMPTY_MODEL;

  const df = new Map<string, number>();
  for (const tokens of docTokens) {
    for (const term of new Set(tokens)) df.set(term, (df.get(term) ?? 0) + 1);
  }

  const idf = new Map<string, number>();
  for (const [term, d] of df) idf.set(term, Math.log((n + 1) / (d + 1)) + 1);

  // corpus profile = summed tf across docs, weighted by idf
  const corpusTf = new Map<string, number>();
  for (const tokens of docTokens) {
    for (const [term, f] of termFreq(tokens)) corpusTf.set(term, (corpusTf.get(term) ?? 0) + f);
  }
  const profile = new Map<string, number>();
  for (const [term, f] of corpusTf) profile.set(term, f * (idf.get(term) ?? 0));

  let sq = 0;
  for (const w of profile.values()) sq += w * w;

  return { profile, profileNorm: Math.sqrt(sq), idf, vocabSize: df.size };
}

export interface AlignmentScore {
  /** cosine similarity in [0, 1] */
  cosine: number;
  /** terms contributing most to the alignment, for the legible reason */
  topTerms: string[];
}

/** Score how well `text` aligns with the memory profile. */
export function scoreAlignment(model: AlignmentModel, text: string): AlignmentScore {
  if (model.profileNorm === 0) return { cosine: 0, topTerms: [] };
  const tf = termFreq(tokenize(text));

  const contrib: Array<[string, number]> = [];
  let dot = 0;
  let sq = 0;
  for (const [term, f] of tf) {
    const idf = model.idf.get(term);
    if (idf === undefined) continue; // term not in memory vocab Рєњ can't align
    const briefW = f * idf;
    sq += briefW * briefW;
    const profileW = model.profile.get(term) ?? 0;
    const c = briefW * profileW;
    if (c > 0) {
      dot += c;
      contrib.push([term, c]);
    }
  }
  const briefNorm = Math.sqrt(sq);
  if (briefNorm === 0 || dot === 0) return { cosine: 0, topTerms: [] };

  const cosine = dot / (briefNorm * model.profileNorm);
  const topTerms = contrib
    .sort((a, b) => b[1] - a[1])
    .slice(0, 3)
    .map(([term]) => term);
  return { cosine, topTerms };
}

```

```typescript
import { type AlignmentModel, scoreAlignment } from "./alignment.js";
import { classifyPattern } from "./pattern.js";
import type { Brief, Pattern, Telemetry } from "./types.js";

export interface PriorityResult {
  score: number;
  reason: string;
  pattern: Pattern;
}

/** Blocker present in `next`. Refines daemon.py's bare-"уГЅ" check so that a
 *  trailing "уГЅ" inside parentheticals (e.g. "(СйютЊЂB уГЅ)") no longer false-fires;
 *  "уГЅ" only counts when followed by concrete content. */
function hasBlocker(next: string): boolean {
  const lower = next.toLowerCase();
  if (lower.includes("block") || lower.includes("stuck") || next.includes("тЇА")) return true;
  return /уГЅ[^\s)№╝Ѕ.сђѓ,№╝ї]/.test(next);
}

function fmtDwell(minutes: number): string {
  return minutes >= 60 ? `${(minutes / 60).toFixed(1)}h` : `${Math.round(minutes)}m`;
}

/** CosineРєњscore scale for memory alignment. Capped so a strong pattern signal
 *  (avoidance +4, stalled +3) still competes Рђћ alignment shouldn't dominate. */
const ALIGN_WEIGHT = 6;
const ALIGN_MIN_COSINE = 0.05;

/**
 * Heuristic priority: memory alignment + pattern weight + blocker bonus Рѕњ
 * defer/done penalty. Returns a composed, human-readable reason carrying the
 * evidence (DESIGN.md: no opaque scores Рђћ the user must be able to audit and
 * override the rank).
 */
export function evaluatePriority(
  brief: Brief,
  tel: Telemetry,
  model: AlignmentModel | null,
): PriorityResult {
  const blob = `${brief.name} ${brief.what} ${brief.next}`;
  let score = 0;
  const reasons: string[] = [];

  if (model) {
    const align = scoreAlignment(model, blob);
    if (align.cosine >= ALIGN_MIN_COSINE) {
      score += align.cosine * ALIGN_WEIGHT;
      const terms = align.topTerms.length ? ` (${align.topTerms.join(", ")})` : "";
      reasons.push(`memory aligned${terms}`);
    }
  }

  const pattern = classifyPattern(tel);
  if (pattern === "avoidance") {
    score += 4;
    reasons.push(
      `avoidance signal (${tel.prompts} prompts, 0 actions over ${fmtDwell(tel.totalMinutes)}) Рђћ needs decision, not work`,
    );
  } else if (pattern === "stalled") {
    score += 3;
    const touch = tel.lastTouchAgeDays !== null ? `, last touch ${tel.lastTouchAgeDays}d` : "";
    reasons.push(`stalled (${tel.prompts} prompts, 0 actions${touch}) Рђћ needs unblock or kill`);
  } else if (pattern === "fresh") {
    score += 1;
    reasons.push("fresh Рђћ no entries yet");
  } else if (pattern === "active") {
    score += 0.5;
  } else if (pattern === "healthy") {
    score -= 1;
    reasons.push("healthy Рђћ in flow, don't interrupt");
  }

  if (brief.status === "deferred") {
    reasons.push(
      brief.deferUntil ? `deferred until ${brief.deferUntil}` : "deferred without condition",
    );
    score -= 2;
  } else if (brief.status === "done") {
    score = -100;
    reasons.push("done");
  }

  if (hasBlocker(brief.next)) {
    score += 1;
    reasons.push("explicit blocker in next");
  }

  return { score, reason: reasons.join("; ") || "no signal", pattern };
}

```

```typescript
import { describe, expect, it } from "vitest";
import { buildAlignmentModel, scoreAlignment, tokenize } from "../src/core/alignment.js";

describe("tokenize", () => {
  it("lowercases latin, drops stopwords and short tokens", () => {
    expect(tokenize("The Widget and go")).toEqual(["widget"]);
  });

  it("explodes CJK runs into character bigrams", () => {
    expect(tokenize("Т│еТёЈтіЏ")).toEqual(["Т│еТёЈ", "ТёЈтіЏ"]);
  });
});

describe("alignment cosine", () => {
  it("scores higher for briefs sharing memory vocabulary", () => {
    const model = buildAlignmentModel([
      "attention router brief telemetry pattern",
      "claude codex session vendor",
    ]);
    const aligned = scoreAlignment(model, "build the attention router telemetry view");
    const unaligned = scoreAlignment(model, "bake a chocolate cake recipe");
    expect(aligned.cosine).toBeGreaterThan(unaligned.cosine);
    expect(aligned.topTerms.length).toBeGreaterThan(0);
  });

  it("aligns Chinese via shared bigrams", () => {
    const model = buildAlignmentModel(["Т│еТёЈтіЏу«АуљєСИјтє│уГќ"]);
    expect(scoreAlignment(model, "У┐ЎТў»Т│еТёЈтіЏУи»ућ▒").cosine).toBeGreaterThan(0);
  });

  it("empty memory yields a zero, well-defined score", () => {
    const model = buildAlignmentModel([]);
    expect(model.vocabSize).toBe(0);
    expect(scoreAlignment(model, "anything").cosine).toBe(0);
  });

  it("single memory doc still produces a usable model", () => {
    const model = buildAlignmentModel(["widget pipeline tokens"]);
    expect(model.vocabSize).toBeGreaterThan(0);
    expect(scoreAlignment(model, "widget").cosine).toBeGreaterThan(0);
  });
});

```

```typescript
import fs from "node:fs";
import path from "node:path";

function template(today: string): string {
  return `---
status: active
last_touch: ${today}
defer_until:
---

## what
СИђтЈЦУ»Ю + why (СИ║С╗ђС╣ѕУ┐ЎС║Ітђ╝тЙЌтЂџ).

## accept
done уџётѕцТЇ« (СИЇТў» todo list).

## next
уј░тюетЇАтюетЊф / СИІСИђТГЦУ»ЦУ»ЋС╗ђС╣ѕ. Т»ЈТгАуд╗т╝ђ session тЅЇТЏ┤Тќ░У┐ЎСИђУАї.
`;
}

export interface ScaffoldResult {
  path: string;
  created: boolean;
}

/**
 * Scaffold `<baseDir>/<name>/brief.md` with the three-section template.
 * Never overwrites an existing brief (briefs are authoritative state).
 */
export function scaffoldBrief(
  name: string,
  baseDir: string,
  today: string = new Date().toISOString().slice(0, 10),
): ScaffoldResult {
  const dir = path.resolve(baseDir, name);
  const file = path.join(dir, "brief.md");
  if (fs.existsSync(file)) return { path: file, created: false };
  fs.mkdirSync(dir, { recursive: true });
  fs.writeFileSync(file, template(today), "utf-8");
  return { path: file, created: true };
}

```

import fs from "node:fs";
import os from "node:os";
import path from "node:path";
import { afterEach, describe, expect, it } from "vitest";
import { scaffoldBrief } from "../src/commands/new.js";

let dir: string;
afterEach(() => {
  if (dir) fs.rmSync(dir, { recursive: true, force: true });
});

describe("scaffoldBrief", () => {
  it("creates projects/<name>/brief.md with the three sections", () => {
    dir = fs.mkdtempSync(path.join(os.tmpdir(), "attend-new-"));
    const res = scaffoldBrief("widget", dir, "2026-05-31");
    expect(res.created).toBe(true);
    expect(res.path).toBe(path.join(dir, "widget", "brief.md"));
    const text = fs.readFileSync(res.path, "utf-8");
    expect(text).toContain("status: active");
    expect(text).toContain("last_touch: 2026-05-31");
    expect(text).toMatch(/## what[\s\S]*## accept[\s\S]*## next/);
  });

  it("never overwrites an existing brief", () => {
    dir = fs.mkdtempSync(path.join(os.tmpdir(), "attend-new-"));
    scaffoldBrief("widget", dir);
    fs.writeFileSync(path.join(dir, "widget", "brief.md"), "MINE", "utf-8");
    const res = scaffoldBrief("widget", dir);
    expect(res.created).toBe(false);
    expect(fs.readFileSync(res.path, "utf-8")).toBe("MINE");
  });
});

```

I found search results related to OpenAI Codex CLI rollout sessions, JSONL format, and SessionMeta. Here's what the search reveals:

## Session Rollout Format

Codex's rollout persistence system records conversation history and session metadata to disk in JSONL format, enabling thread resumption, allowing inspection of conversation state for debugging, and providing a durable audit trail of agent interactions.

## File Structure

Codex persists conversation state to rollout files: newline-delimited JSON files where each line contains a RolloutLine wrapping a RolloutItem. These files are stored in a hierarchical date-based structure under the sessions directory, typically ~/.codex/sessions/YYYY/MM/DD/rollout-TIMESTAMP-UUID.jsonl.

## SessionMeta and cwd

SessionMeta captures git_sha, git_branch, and git_origin_url from the session metadata. Additionally, session metadata includes the cwd (current working directory).

## Record Schema Evolution

Rollout files written by previous releases contain bare SessionMeta/ResponseItem JSON and cannot deserialize into the newer RolloutLine enum. A fallback that understands the old format is needed when RolloutLine parsing fails.

The search results point to the GitHub OpenAI Codex repository which contains detailed discussions about the rollout file format and schema, but the specific complete schema definition wasn't fully detailed in these results.

# Codex Rollout JSONL Schema

Based on the documentation provided, here's the schema structure:

## RolloutLine Wrapper

Each line wraps a `RolloutItem` and adds metadata:
- **timestamp**: UTC timestamp for the event
- **payload**: The actual `RolloutItem` (discriminated by type)

The document states: "The `RolloutLine` type wraps a `RolloutItem` and adds a UTC timestamp for each persisted event."

## RolloutItem Type Discriminator

The `RolloutItem` enum defines these primary types:

| Type | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `ResponseItem` | "Raw model responses and tool calls" containing role, content, and turn markers |
| `EventMsg` | "Protocol-level events" including UserMessage, AgentMessage, TokenCount, lifecycle events |
| `SessionMeta` | "Session-level metadata" with id, source, cwd, model_provider, cli_version |
| `TurnContext` | "Snapshot of turn settings" capturing model, approval_policy, sandbox_policy |
| `Compacted` | Summary items from history compaction |

## SessionMeta Payload Fields

Key fields include:
- **id**: Session identifier
- **source**: Origin of the session
- **cwd**: Current working directory
- **model_provider**: LLM provider
- **cli_version**: CLI build version
- **git_sha, git_branch, git_origin_url**: Git context captured from SessionMeta

## Message and Execution Representation

The document indicates events are represented as `EventMsg` types containing discriminated variants like `UserMessage`, `AgentMessage`, and tool execution events. However, **the document does not provide concrete JSON field names or example JSONL lines** for these payloads.

For detailed field mappings and example JSON, you would need to consult the actual source files referenced (e.g., `codex-rs/protocol/src/protocol.rs`).
```

```text
# RolloutItem and RolloutLine Analysis

## RolloutItem Definition

**Serde Attributes:**
```rust
#[serde(tag = "type", content = "payload", rename_all = "snake_case")]
```

**Variants with Rename Strings:**
- `SessionMeta` Рєњ `"session_meta"`
- `ResponseItem` Рєњ `"response_item"`
- `Compacted` Рєњ `"compacted"`
- `TurnContext` Рєњ `"turn_context"`
- `EventMsg` Рєњ `"event_msg"`

**Exact Definition:**
```rust
#[derive(Serialize, Deserialize, Debug, Clone, JsonSchema, TS)]

```typescript
import fs from "node:fs";
import path from "node:path";
import type { RawSession } from "../types.js";
import type { SessionSource } from "./index.js";

/**
 * Codex CLI rollout transcripts (~/.codex/sessions/YYYY/MM/DD/rollout-*.jsonl).
 *
 * Schema (openai/codex, codex-rs/protocol): each line is a RolloutLine Рђћ
 *   { "timestamp": "...", "type": <RolloutItem tag>, "payload": {...} }
 * with RolloutItem adjacently tagged, rename_all = snake_case:
 *   session_meta | response_item | event_msg | turn_context | compacted
 * `payload` of a response_item is an OpenAI Responses API item.
 *
 * Mapping to attend's vendor-neutral RawSession (parallels the Claude adapter):
 *   - cwd      Рєљ session_meta.payload.cwd
 *   - prompts  Рєљ response_item message with role "user" carrying input_text
 *   - actions  Рєљ response_item function_call | local_shell_call | custom_tool_call
 *
 * NOTE: implemented from the documented schema; no local Codex install was
 * available to validate against a real rollout file. The mapping is conservative
 * (counts only unambiguous user turns and tool executions) and degrades to an
 * empty result rather than fabricating telemetry (DESIGN.md invariant 3). When a
 * real sample exists, confirm the field names below.
 */

const ACTION_ITEM_TYPES = new Set(["function_call", "local_shell_call", "custom_tool_call"]);

interface RolloutLine {
  timestamp?: string;
  type?: string;
  payload?: ResponseItem & { cwd?: string };
  // tolerate bare (pre-RolloutLine) records that omit the wrapper
  cwd?: string;
  role?: string;
  content?: unknown;
}

interface ResponseItem {
  type?: string;
  role?: string;
  content?: unknown;
  cwd?: string;
}

function parseTs(value: unknown): number | null {
  if (typeof value !== "string") return null;
  const t = Date.parse(value);
  return Number.isNaN(t) ? null : t;
}

function isUserPrompt(item: ResponseItem): boolean {
  if (item.type !== "message" || item.role !== "user") return false;
  const c = item.content;
  if (typeof c === "string") return c.trim() !== "";
  if (Array.isArray(c)) {
    return c.some((b) => {
      if (!b || typeof b !== "object") return false;
      const block = b as { type?: string; text?: string };
      return block.type === "input_text" && !!block.text && block.text.trim() !== "";
    });
  }
  return false;
}

/** Parse one Codex rollout transcript's text into a normalized session (pure, testable). */
export function parseCodexTranscript(file: string, raw: string): RawSession {
  const session: RawSession = {
    path: file,
    vendor: "codex",
    cwd: null,
    firstTs: null,
    lastTs: null,
    prompts: 0,
    actions: 0,
  };
  for (const line of raw.split(/\r?\n/)) {
    if (!line.trim()) continue;
    let obj: RolloutLine;
    try {
      obj = JSON.parse(line) as RolloutLine;
    } catch {
      continue;
    }

    const ts = parseTs(obj.timestamp);
    if (ts !== null) {
      if (session.firstTs === null) session.firstTs = ts;
      session.lastTs = ts;
    }

    // Normalize wrapped vs. bare records into (kind, item).
    let kind = obj.type;
    let item: ResponseItem | undefined = obj.payload;
    if (item === undefined) {
      // bare record: infer kind from its own shape
      if (obj.cwd !== undefined && obj.type === undefined) kind = "session_meta";
      else if (obj.type) {
        kind = "response_item";
        item = { type: obj.type, role: obj.role, content: obj.content };
      }
    }

    if (kind === "session_meta") {
      const cwd = item?.cwd ?? obj.cwd;
      if (session.cwd === null && cwd) session.cwd = cwd;
    } else if (kind === "response_item" && item) {
      if (isUserPrompt(item)) session.prompts += 1;
      else if (item.type && ACTION_ITEM_TYPES.has(item.type)) session.actions += 1;
    }
  }
  return session;
}

function findJsonl(root: string): string[] {
  const out: string[] = [];
  const walk = (dir: string) => {
    let entries: fs.Dirent[];
    try {
      entries = fs.readdirSync(dir, { withFileTypes: true });
    } catch {
      return;
    }
    for (const e of entries) {
      const full = path.join(dir, e.name);
      if (e.isDirectory()) walk(full);
      else if (e.isFile() && e.name.endsWith(".jsonl")) out.push(full);
    }
  };
  walk(root);
  return out;
}

export class CodexSource implements SessionSource {
  readonly vendor = "codex";

  constructor(private readonly sessionsDir: string) {}

  scan(): RawSession[] {
    if (!fs.existsSync(this.sessionsDir)) return [];
    return findJsonl(this.sessionsDir).map((f) => {
      try {
        return parseCodexTranscript(f, fs.readFileSync(f, "utf-8"));
      } catch {
        return {
          path: f,
          vendor: "codex" as const,
          cwd: null,
          firstTs: null,
          lastTs: null,
          prompts: 0,
          actions: 0,
        };
      }
    });
  }
}

```

```typescript
import { describe, expect, it } from "vitest";
import { parseCodexTranscript } from "../src/core/vendor/codex.js";

function jsonl(...lines: object[]): string {
  return lines.map((l) => JSON.stringify(l)).join("\n");
}

describe("parseCodexTranscript (RolloutLine schema)", () => {
  it("reads cwd from session_meta, counts user prompts and tool-call actions", () => {
    const raw = jsonl(
      {
        timestamp: "2026-05-01T10:00:00Z",
        type: "session_meta",
        payload: { id: "abc", cwd: "/home/u/proj", cli_version: "0.1" },
      },
      {
        timestamp: "2026-05-01T10:01:00Z",
        type: "response_item",
        payload: { type: "message", role: "user", content: [{ type: "input_text", text: "do X" }] },
      },
      {
        timestamp: "2026-05-01T10:02:00Z",
        type: "response_item",
        payload: { type: "local_shell_call", command: ["ls"] },
      },
      {
        timestamp: "2026-05-01T10:03:00Z",
        type: "response_item",
        payload: { type: "function_call", name: "apply_patch", arguments: "{}" },
      },
    );
    const s = parseCodexTranscript("rollout.jsonl", raw);
    expect(s.vendor).toBe("codex");
    expect(s.cwd).toBe("/home/u/proj");
    expect(s.prompts).toBe(1);
    expect(s.actions).toBe(2);
    expect(s.firstTs).toBe(Date.parse("2026-05-01T10:00:00Z"));
    expect(s.lastTs).toBe(Date.parse("2026-05-01T10:03:00Z"));
  });

  it("does not count assistant messages or non-action response items", () => {
    const raw = jsonl(
      { type: "response_item", payload: { type: "message", role: "assistant", content: [{ type: "output_text", text: "hi" }] } },
      { type: "response_item", payload: { type: "reasoning", summary: [] } },
      { type: "event_msg", payload: { type: "token_count", total: 10 } },
    );
    const s = parseCodexTranscript("r.jsonl", raw);
    expect(s.prompts).toBe(0);
    expect(s.actions).toBe(0);
  });

  it("tolerates bare (pre-RolloutLine) records and malformed lines", () => {
    const raw = [
      "garbage",
      JSON.stringify({ cwd: "/bare/proj" }),
      JSON.stringify({ type: "message", role: "user", content: [{ type: "input_text", text: "hey" }] }),
    ].join("\n");
    const s = parseCodexTranscript("r.jsonl", raw);
    expect(s.cwd).toBe("/bare/proj");
    expect(s.prompts).toBe(1);
  });
});

```

```text
126	## Research touchstones
127	
128	- **Steel, P. (2007)** Рђћ *Arousal, avoidant, and decisional procrastinators: do they exist?* Three procrastination types; foundational for the avoidance pattern definition. Identified behavioral-measurement gap: most procrastination research is self-report, telemetry is largely unexplored. This is the niche the telemetry component fills.
129	- **Gollwitzer, P. (1999)** Рђћ *Implementation intentions.* Pre-committing "if X then Y" at cheap time reduces decision-point friction. Direct rationale for the `next` line discipline.
130	- **Rosenbaum (2014)** Рђћ *Pre-crastination.* Doing the easy thing first to free working memory is a real mechanism, not always avoidance. Distinguishes by whether the easy task ends in 1-2 hours; if longer, it's likely the avoidance pattern.
131	- **Karpathy LLM Wiki pattern** Рђћ `wiki/hot.md` of ~500 words auto-loaded at session start. Brief is our hot-cache analog.
132	- **Slock.ai** Рђћ Persistent channels with transient message streams. Structural inspiration for brief/session split.
133	
134	## v0 Рєњ v1 roadmap
135	
136	- [ ] Codex JSONL parser (need a sample of Codex's schema; user has it via CLI but not yet active)
137	- [ ] `attend split <jsonl-path>` CLI: LLM extracts open follow-up threads Рєњ N candidate briefs to review
138	- [ ] `attend new <project>` to scaffold `projects/<project>/brief.md`
139	- [ ] LLM-based priority option (env-flag)
140	- [ ] Refined blocker regex (only `уГЅ[тЁиСйЊтєЁт«╣]`, not bare `уГЅ` char Рђћ currently triggers false positives on parentheticals like "(СйютЊЂB уГЅ)")
141	- [ ] Dwell distribution heatmap per brief
142	- [ ] In-browser brief edit (currently file-system only)
143	
144	## Out of scope (likely permanently)
145	
146	- Multi-user collaboration (Slock-style server/channel/DM)
147	- Real-time push / SSE / websocket
148	- Chat UI in the dashboard (sessions live in Claude Code / Codex)
149	- Hierarchical task trees (briefs are flat; relations live in the `next` line as references)
150	- Cloud sync (vault is local; OneDrive sync of the vault is incidental, not a feature)
151	
152	## How a future session onboards
153	
154	If you (Claude / Codex / human) are picking this up cold:
155	
156	1. Read this document end-to-end (including the v1 pivot section below).
157	2. Read `README.md` for run instructions.
158	3. Read `brief.md` for current `next`.
159	4. Open http://localhost:5050 if the app is running (`npx attend` or `npm start`).
160	5. Pick from the brief's `next` and start.
161	
162	Do not add organizational layers unless you've checked them against invariant 5. Do not change telemetry output to be more "motivating" Рђћ that's invariant 3 violation; see Steel research.
163	
164	## v1: Node/npx pivot (2026-05)
165	
166	v0 was a single-file Python/Flask `daemon.py` run via `python daemon.py`. v1 reimplements it as a **Node + TypeScript** package distributable via `npx` (zero-install: `npx attend` / `npx github:you/attend`, boots a local server, opens the browser). The original is preserved under `legacy/` for reference.
167	
168	**Why the change.** The goal was cross-platform (Win + macOS) zero-install distribution. Two product forms were weighed:
169	
170	- **Tauri (rejected).** Considered, then rejected: Tauri bundles a native installer you *download and install* Рђћ the opposite of zero-install. It also renders into an embedded WebView, not a localhost page you open in any browser, so it would have broken the "single localhost polling surface" form. (Note: this supersedes the older "desktop app Рђћ engineering overhead" rejection note above for the same reason, just made concrete.)
171	- **Node CLI + local web server (chosen).** Matches `npx serve`-style distribution: publish once (or run from GitHub), `npx` fetches-and-runs, no permanent install, browser is the UI. Node runs on Win/macOS uniformly. This *restores* the original localhost single-surface form Рђћ only the runtime changed (Python Рєњ Node), to make `npx` distribution possible.
172	
173	**What did NOT change.** All five design invariants hold. The memory model is unchanged Рђћ still Claude Code's per-project memory (`~/.claude/projects/*/memory/MEMORY.md`), just generalized from one hardcoded file to auto-discovering and unioning all per-project memories. The pattern definitions, priority heuristic, descriptive-telemetry constraint, and spawn-by-copy behavior are ported 1:1 (one refinement: the bare-"уГЅ" blocker false-positive from the roadmap is fixed in `priority.ts`).
174	
175	**Architecture.** `src/core/` is pure domain logic with no server dependency (unit-tested with vitest); `src/server.ts` (Hono) and `src/ui/` are a thin render layer; `src/core/vendor/` holds the `SessionSource` extension point (Claude impl + Codex stub). Adding a vendor = one new `SessionSource` Рђћ the rest stays vendor-neutral (invariant 4). See `README.md` for the file map and dev commands.
176
```
